entries.title: Cubs learn art of stealing bases from ‘zen’ master Quintin Berry
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> third-base coach Quintin Berry is an accomplished base stealer. He once stole 21 consecutive bases without being caught and was 5-for-5 in stolen-base attempts in the postseason, including 3-for-3 with the Red Sox when they won the World Series in 2013.</p><p>Berry talks frequently with Pete Crow-Armstrong and other Cubs baserunners about the art of stealing of a bag. First-base coach Jose Javier works with them more about the technical aspects, he said. But Berry, 42, focuses on the zen.</p><p>“I’m more of a peace of mind [guy] — peace for [Crow-Armstrong],’’ he said. “Just like, what is he thinking, what’s he doing with his body. How to keep him relaxed and be confident.</p><p>“A lot of times, guys want to be quick. They’re trying so hard. I think the harder you try, the more tense you get, the more locked up, and then you see things that aren’t there, and then you get real passive, or you get real jumpy.</p><p>“So I’m just trying to make guys feel confident, confident and comfortable.’’</p><p>When Berry was with the Brewers, they had the third highest stolen-base percentage in the majors. So far this season, the Cubs are 7-for-7. Nico Hoerner has four steals, Crow-Armstrong two and Matt Shaw one.</p>
entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Cubs third-base coach Quintin Berry feels boxed in with new MLB rules
entries.summary: <p>Quintin Berry says he’s thinking about bringing a glove with him when he takes his place in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a>’ third-base coaching box.</p><p>With Major League Baseball informing teams that base coaches can no longer stray beyond the boundaries of the box — which for years has functioned as just a general notion of where a coach should park — Berry believes he needs the protection.</p><p>Under the new rules, can he pack a leather piece? Berry said he plans to look into it. But now that he can no longer stand beyond the outer boundary of the box — which is just 90 feet from home plate — he and other base coaches fear their jobs have just become expo-nentially more dangerous.</p><p>There’s little wiggle room when a 110 mph foul ball is hurtling your way. It would seem only a matter of time that the increase in exit velocities, with all of baseball’s emphasis on launch angles, bat speed and barreling the ball, could result in injuries. There were a few close calls in spring training.</p><p>Berry, who played 13 seasons in the big leagues, including eight games with the Cubs in 2015, during which he had one plate appearance and was used primarily as a pinch runner, is in his second season as third-base coach. He came with manager Craig Counsell from Milwaukee, where he had been first-base coach for four seasons.</p><p>“It’s funny,’’ Berry said earlier this week, before the Cubs left for Cleveland and a three-game weekend set against the Guardians, “I’ve talked with a couple of third-base coaches about bringing a glove out there, have a glove on just in case, because it’s like you’re never out of harm’s way.</p><p>“There’s no rule against it, supposedly. It’s a matter of who’s going to do it first.’’</p><p>MLB announced in February it would be enforcing the boundaries in response to complaints that coaches were occupying positions that would allow them a more advantageous view of a pitcher’s grip. If a coach knew what a pitcher was about to throw, he could relay that information to the hitter. Sign-stealing and similar tactics have long been legally part of the game, as long as electronic technology is not employed.</p><p>According to The Athletic, before Game  7 of the World Series between the Blue Jays and Dodgers last fall, both teams were warned that coaches had to stay in the boxes.</p><p>The matter was discussed among big-league managers during the winter meetings. MLB’s joint competition committee then took up the topic, and the change was announced after the owners’ meetings.</p><p>“It’s probably a good thing just to regulate that,’’ Yankees manager Bret Boone told The Athletic at the time. “. . . I knew something was coming. Overall, I think it’s probably a good thing, just for the competitiveness of the game.’’</p><p>Umpires have been instructed to first give coaches who are violating the rules a warning. A second warning can result in ejection.</p><p>Berry would prefer to focus on staying in one piece. He already wears a batting helmet. He understands the concerns about sign-stealing but isn’t sure the tradeoff is worth it.</p><p>MLB has said coaches can retreat backward toward foul territory — the box is not bracketed in the back. But then a coach can’t do his job, Berry said.</p><p>“You see our foul territory — we have [the boxes] exactly,’’ he said. “You’re kind of anchored right in there, near the hitters’ box.</p><p>“It’s scary, man. I mean, every coach feels like they’re in a bad spot, but we’re trying to get used to it and see what happens.”</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 entries.title: Man shot during argument in Grand Boulevard
entries.summary: <p>A man was hospitalized after he was shot during an argument in Grand Boulevard Thursday afternoon, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The man, 42, was arguing with another man when that man pulled out a gun and shot him several times, police said. The shooting occurred in the 4500 block of South Drexel Boulevard at 5:34 p.m., police said.</p><p>Shot in his left hand, right shoulder and chest, the man was taken to University of Chicago Medical Center in serious condition, police said.</p><p>No one is in custody.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Chicago's top cop stands by his officers interactions with ICE during Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>Chicago police Supt. Larry Snelling is standing by his department’s actions during ramped-up immigration enforcement last year, telling residents his officers were “out there working to keep communities safe.”</p><p>Snelling joined the Community Commission for Public Safety and Accountability for a “special hearing” Thursday to discuss his department's interactions with federal immigration agents.</p><p>Dozens of people attended, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/08/residents-voice-frustrations-with-chicago-police-interactions-during-immigration-raids-who-protects-us" target="_blank" ><u>some accusing Chicago police of illegally coordinating with immigration agents</u></a> during President Donald Trump’s aggressive deportation campaign.</p><p>A similar meeting back in January also was packed, but this time around those attending heard responses from Snelling directly, leading to some tense moments in the crowd.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> "This is the response that I expected. … It’s easy to blame CPD when you’re looking for somebody to blame.”</p><p>The meeting ended to chants of “CPD, KKK, I-C-E, they’re all the same" as well as multiple people being escorted out of the building.</p><p>Though Snelling stood firmly by his department, he told commissioners if officers were found to be violating Chicago’s Welcoming City Ordinance they would be held accountable.</p><p>The commission voted Thursday to recommend the city’s Office of the Inspector General conduct an audit of possible violations. It’s common for Chicago police to work closely with federal agencies on criminal investigations.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8f0000" name="image-8f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9090e44/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F70%2F9263fe4e49cf926f091378e85005%2Fsnellingice-260403-34.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f5d42f2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F70%2F9263fe4e49cf926f091378e85005%2Fsnellingice-260403-34.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/42bfef9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F70%2F9263fe4e49cf926f091378e85005%2Fsnellingice-260403-34.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago Public School security officials forcibly remove activists who disrupted a meeting of the Community Commission for Public Safety and Accountability on Thurdsay. California Ave., on Thursday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>They used U.S. Customs and Border Protection’s facial recognition technology to identify<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" > the man accused of killing Loyola University student Sheridan Gorman</a>. This week, they <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/02/chicago-ohare-ice-depaul-crime-oneill-burke-spit" >arrested a DePaul University student accused of spitting</a> on an U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent at O’Hare airport, though the charges have been dropped.</p><p>But state and city law prohibit Chicago police from assisting with federal immigration enforcement. Still, there were several incidents last year that raised questions about whether that line had been crossed.</p><p>Snelling has argued more education is needed around what these laws mean in practice.</p><p>“No one wrote the ordinance and the law with this type of immigration enforcement in mind,” Snelling said Thursday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-680000" name="image-680000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1fa43e1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fbf%2F0e3404464f6a8f0aaa523bd422f9%2Fsnellingice-260403-25.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a50d16d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fbf%2F0e3404464f6a8f0aaa523bd422f9%2Fsnellingice-260403-25.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d0bb987/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fbf%2F0e3404464f6a8f0aaa523bd422f9%2Fsnellingice-260403-25.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="CPD Supt. Larry Snelling answers questions at a meeting of the Community Commission for Public Safety and Accountability at Thomas Kelly College Preparatory High School, 4136 S. California Ave., on Thursday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In June, Chicago police responded to a 911 call from an ICE worker requesting assistance with “crowd control,” during a civil immigration raid outside the agency’s South Loop office.</p><p>Advocates and some alderpersons clashed with federal agents after at least 10 people were arrested during routine immigration check-ins. Witnesses reported Chicago police appearing to “stand guard” for ICE vehicles and accompanying agents in and out of the building.</p><p>City Council members said body-worn camera footage indicated some level of coordination with the feds, but without further investigation, they couldn't determine if sanctuary policies were violated.</p><p>As of March, the Civilian Office of Police Accountability has been put in charge of investigating officers accused of violating Chicago’s sanctuary laws.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" A commander initially told officers to hold back, though in a press conference days later, Snelling argued Chicago police were there “throughout the entire event.”</p><p>“Miss Martinez was shot by federal agents. … When that occurred, our officers were there on the scene,” Snelling said Thursday. “There was civil unrest. Our officers were there to keep the crowd separated. You say we protected ICE? ICE needed no protection.”</p><p>Block Club Chicago reported that Snelling had refused to attend the Thursday meeting if experts on police reform and immigration were allowed to speak alongside him. entries.title: Horoscope for Friday, April 03, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon alert</h2><p>After 8 a.m. Chicago time, there are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions. The moon is in Scorpio.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>TGIF, we're thankful to get here. However, this day has its challenges. It's a poor day for important discussions about financial matters, shared property, inheritances, wills, insurance issues and discussing the wealth of your partner or someone else. Table these topics for another day.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Life is a bit confusing today because you're very determined about something — dare we say obsessed? (We did.) Obsessive thinking can obscure your ability to see the big picture. To keep the peace, tread carefully with parents and authority figures.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today you might be excited about a secret love affair or some kind of secret pleasure that few people know about. You might have made a commitment to a particular cause that is political or personal. You might even have secret travel plans. The possibilities are endless.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Relations with a friend or a group might be emotional today. You might have to confront someone. Ironically, you might even have to confront yourself. Be careful not to let someone else manipulate you or use guilt or jealousy as a technique.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Tread carefully today because you might bump heads with parents, bosses, teachers, VIPs and the police. You have strong feelings about something today. It's possible this relates to a domestic situation or a partner. Don't push your luck. Step back. Give everything some breathing space. Be conciliatory.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Steer clear of arguments today and resist the urge to make someone agree with your way of thinking because that is not your best choice. People have strong feelings and strong opinions today — to an almost ruthless degree. Don't get caught in the crossfire.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Your best option today is to work alone because everyone has a strong opinion, especially about financial matters. Romance is also a passionate issue today. Be careful. Postpone what you can until tomorrow, which is a lovely day.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You have strong opinions and feelings today, and you might make your case. However, you are best served today by working alone and setting aside some time for some friendly, easygoing socializing — romantically or with your kids or related to sports. Keep things light.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Do your best to be patient and diplomatic with co-workers and care workers today because it's easy to get into a power struggle with someone. Don't do this because it will be regrettable. (Tomorrow is a fabulous day!)</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Avoid dicey communications with romantic partners today. Likewise, parents should take it easy dealing with their kids. Resist making things a black-or-white issue. Be smart, and postpone important discussions until tomorrow, which is a lovely, easygoing day. You won't regret doing this.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Emotions are running high today because people are opinionated and stubborn. (You know who you are.) Be diplomatic when dealing with family members today. Don’t make a big deal about things. If you sidestep these issues, you can actually get a lot done.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're pumped with energy because fiery Mars is in your sign. You're also ready to speak because Mercury is in your sign. However, everyone is highly opinionated today — including you. Acting on sudden urges and impulses might not be a good influence for you in the long run. Take a breath so that you can give everything a sober, second thought.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor Eddie Murphy (1961) shares your birthday. You are energetic and charismatic, and you have strong views. You're also persuasive and perceptive. This year you will reap the rewards of your past efforts. Expect power and leadership. You might be in the spotlight. Acknowledgment, kudos and awards could come your way. See opportunities and act on them.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Blackhawks' loss to Oilers officially eliminates them from playoff contention
entries.summary: <p>EDMONTON, Alberta — Even the most optimistic <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> players gave up on the playoffs weeks ago, but the inevitable outcome is now guaranteed.</p><p>A 3-1 loss Thursday against the Oilers officially eliminated the Hawks from playoff contention for a seventh consecutive season.</p><p>The Hawks' odds of ending that drought in 2026-27 might be better than they've been in a while, but they still won't be high. In the meantime, they have just six games left in 2025-26.</p><p>Rookie forward Nick Lardis scored the Hawks' lone goal — his fourth in seven games — during a third-period push, but he later left injured after a Teuvo Teravainen shot drilled his hand.</p><p>Goalie Spencer Knight saved 31 of 32 shots but missed about five minutes of the second period due to an equipment issue, during which the Oilers took the lead on an Adam Henrique breakaway goal on Arvid Soderblom. It marked just the second time in 76 games this season the Hawks have made an in-game goaltending change.</p><p>"I wouldn’t say we didn’t have opportunities," coach Jeff Blashill said, noting that the team's internal analytics actually gave the Hawks a 7-6 advantage in Grade A chances. "I would say we need to shoot more."</p><p>The Hawks have beaten the Oilers just once in their last 12 meetings, which shouldn't be surprising given the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/02/blackhawks-oilers-contrast-connor-murphy-jason-dickinson-winning" target="_blank" >talent gap</a> between the two teams.</p><p>"It felt like we were close but not close enough," Wyatt Kaiser said. "We were kind of chasing after it the whole game."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Blashill said Burakovsky's turnover on the Oilers' first goal factored into his demotion to the 4th line.<br><br>"He’s not feeling it right now. Unfortunately, he’s trying hard, he cares, he wants to be a good player, it’s just not going right now... Sometimes my job as a coach is to…</p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2039920320837308585?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 3, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>Slaggert’s search</b></h3><p>Landon Slaggert has established himself as a regular among Hawks forwards during the second half of the season after bouncing in and out during the first half.</p><p>"What's been able to keep me in the lineup is my competitiveness, my stick, getting in those areas on the forecheck and forcing some turnovers," Slaggert said recently. "I think that's been a strong point in my game."</p><p>What hasn't kept him in the lineup, however, is offense — or in his case, a lack of offense. He's mired in a 16-game point drought and has just three goals and three assists in 48 games overall.</p><p>He has generated several dangerous scoring chances recently, including a first-period breakaway Thursday, but has failed to convert any.</p><p>"The next step is finishing those off, because I know I do have the ability to produce a little more than I am right now," he said, adding that it will be a summer training focus.</p><p>"I want to watch a lot of net-front [video]. I find myself around that blue paint a lot. Watching [Tyler Bertuzzi] and how good he is around the net, it's [about] not getting tied up with the 'D.' Obviously getting in the goalie's eyes is important, but he finds a way to almost sneak behind these defenders and make himself available."</p><h3><b>Gajan signs</b></h3><p>Hawks prospect goalie Adam Gajan made a somewhat unexpected decision Wednesday to turn pro and sign an entry-level contract.</p><p>Gajan's sophomore season at Minnesota-Duluth (19-13-1 record, .908 save percentage) went much better than his freshman season (7-12-1, .885), but he might end up in the ECHL next season with Spencer Knight, Arvid Soderblom, Drew Commesso and Stan Berezhnoy ahead of him on the depth chart.</p><p>With the 35th pick in 2023, the Hawks made Gajan the highest-drafted goalie in that class, ahead of Michael Hrabal (Mammoth) and Trey Augustine (Red Wings) — two guys who enjoyed better college careers. Then again, pro success is the only thing the Hawks will care about.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Bears coach Ben Johnson remains a tough grader on QB Caleb Williams, and that's what he needs
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The groundswell of enthusiasm for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams has surged to the point that some of the fan base won’t tolerate even a syllable of criticism coming off a playoff run and a season of viral highlights.</p><p>That’s not how it works at Halas Hall.</p><p>The objective feedback Williams received from coach Ben Johnson was the foundation for his growth last season, and unforgettable plays <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/01/18/bears-qb-caleb-williams-forces-overtime-with-ridiculous-pass-but-rams-win-20-17" >like his fourth-and-four touchdown pass against the Rams</a> won’t change that. Johnson is a much tougher grader than the public, and that will be essential to Williams taking a bigger step this season.</p><p>In an interview with the Sun-Times during the NFL annual meeting, Johnson was bullish on Williams’ future and mentioned several indicators that he’s on the right track, saying there is “no doubt” he is primed to take another significant step, but by no means saw last season as an arrival.</p><p>“Oh, we’re just starting his development,” Johnson said. “I’m not saying this is a project, but this is going to be a year-in, year-out improvement that we’re going to see from this guy.”</p><p>Johnson had a list of areas Williams must attack this offseason, whether in his personal workouts or during spring practices, and it starts with sharper throws.</p><p>He went back to a standard he set for Williams going into training camp last year of completing 70% of his passes, saying, “That’s what elite passing offenses do, and we were far from that goal.”</p><p>Williams completed 58.1% of his passes, last in the league, which led to him finishing 22<sup>nd</sup> in passer rating at 90.1. The only player who hit 70% was fellow 2024 first-round pick Drake Maye at 72% for the Patriots.</p><p>On the other side of the ledger, Williams set the franchise record with 3,942 passing yards, threw 27 touchdown passes with just seven interceptions and had seven fourth-quarter comebacks, including the playoff win over the Packers.</p><p>Nonetheless, Johnson plans to go through film with Williams and show him 80-100 passes that could have been completions with better timing and a more accurate throw. That’s not including drops by wide receivers. If Williams had hit on even 40 of those plays Johnson has in mind, his completion percentage would’ve jumped to 65.1.</p><p>Passing accuracy is clear-cut to Johnson, but timing and pocket presence are a little more ambiguous. The challenge is that he wants more “traditional quarterback play” from Williams — such as making some of the quick, clockwork completions Johnson depends on when he designs a play — without scrubbing out the artistry and unpredictability that makes him a one-of-a-kind weapon.</p><p>They’ll work together to find an ideal blend.</p><p>“As we watch some of this stuff together in the springtime, we can look at [whether] we stay in that pocket for another half-second and deliver the football to the primary receiver, knowing that we’re going to get hit,” Johnson said. “Or do we need to keep doing what we’re doing, which is bailing just a little bit earlier and turning it into one of those streetball plays?</p><p>“That’s a healthy balance that we’ve got to talk about, and as long as he and I see it the same way, we’re going to be just fine.”</p><p>That alignment is the key. So many dominant teams have had it, and it has eluded the Bears. One of their greatest hopes in hiring Johnson last year was that they’d finally establish a powerful coach-quarterback connection.</p><p>For the most part, it requires Williams to embrace hard coaching and an emphasis on Jared Goff-like precision in some facets, but Johnson must move his way as well by accepting and incorporating Williams’ creativity into his offense. Both seem to be on board with that idea.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: In a world of one-hit wonders, NFL coaches suspect Bears' Ben Johnson is built to last
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> coach Ben Johnson doesn’t want to be a one-hit wonder.</p><p>Neither did Matt Nagy, who went 12-4 in 2018 and never posted another winning season with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/01/bears-chairman-george-mccaskey-coach-ben-johnson-rip-packers-fine-back-it-up-virginia-owners-meetings-green-bay" target="_blank" >Bears</a>. Nor did Dick Jauron, who went 13-3 in 2001 in what proved to be his only successful year with the franchise.</p><p>In the NFL, though, it happens more often than not. That’s why there are only five head coaches left in the league who were hired by their teams before 2021.</p><p>One season of success doesn’t guarantee a dynasty — or even a long career.</p><p>“You want to win the Super Bowl, and you want to win multiple Super Bowls,” said Dan Campbell, who just finished his fifth season with the Lions. “You want to do what [former Patriots coach Bill] Belichick did, and [Chiefs coach] Andy Reid, all these guys, if you can. That’s what we’re in it for.”</p><p>Campbell detailed the ups and downs of his Lions teams, which started at 3-13-1 in 2021 and peaked with a 15-2 record in 2024 and missed the playoffs last year.</p><p>“There’s nothing sicker than when you’re finding wins, you’re winning, but deep down you’re like, ‘We don’t match up with anybody in the playoffs … ” Campbell said. “So you’re always trying to improve and get over the hump.”</p><p>Campbell, who mentored Johnson in Detroit, thinks the Bears head coach is built to do just that.</p><p>“Ben’ll be fine,” he said. “Ben’s highly driven, he’s very smart. Highly competitive.”</p><p>The longest-tenured coach in the NFL agrees.</p><p>“He’ll do a great job with that,” Reid said. “The way he’s wired, he’s not going to lose focus.”</p><p>That’s one thing general manager Ryan Poles noticed in his first season with Johnson. Every day, he said, Johnson instilled a sense of urgency to get better.</p><p>“We talk about standards a lot — setting a high, high bar for yourself as an individual and a football team,” Poles said. “You’re constantly chasing. It makes you stay focused on the process more than the results.”</p><p>The chase only intensifies now, whether Johnson wants to think of it that way or not. It didn’t take Johnson long this offseason to grow tired of being congratulated for a 11-6 season and the Bears’ first playoff win in 15 years.</p><p>“You don't want to hear it anymore, all right?” Johnson said. “It was great for the first week, but now our sights are turned.”</p><p>The Bears have taken steps to fight the sense of self-satisfaction within their roster — they’ve turned it over, particularly on defense. Gone are both starting safeties, one starting cornerback and a starting inside linebacker. Safety Kevin Byard, a captain the last two years, signed with the Patriots. Receiver DJ Moore and linebacker Tremaine Edmunds, captains in 2024, were traded and released, respectively.</p><p>“When you lose so many guys to free agency and you bring in a new crew, that does help with the entitlement and complacency,” Johnson said.</p><p>He’ll have less time around those players, though. In the NFL, first-year coaches are granted extra offseason work with their new teams. That’s not the case in Year 2. Jaguars coach Liam Coen, who in Year 1 went 13-4 and finished second in Coach of the Year voting, has already adjusted accordingly.</p><p>“We need to be as intentional as ever with the way we attack each day …” he said. “We’ve got no time to waste.”</p><p>He’s prepared for the possibility, if not perhaps the likelihood, that the Jaguars won’t win 13 games again this year. But that doesn’t preclude them being better than they were last year.</p><p>“I don’t know what those results are,” he said. “I do know that if we’re chasing those results we’re not going to be living in the moment.”</p><p>As the 49ers’ defensive coordinator last year, Robert Saleh watched head coach Kyle Shanahan navigate another tumultuous season — but still finish 12-5. Since 2019, Shanahan has won 10 or more games five times — and only six games in each of the other two. All the while, he said, the 49ers were moving generally in the right direction.</p><p>Johnson, he said, can do that, too.</p><p>“Ben is a very, very talented football coach — he’s one of the better ones in football,” said Saleh, the new Titans head coach. “There’s no doubt he’s going to keep that thing going for a while.”</p><p>History shows, though, how much easier that is to say than to do.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.title: Dear Abby: Long relationship has fluctuated over the years
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a situation with this guy I've known for five years. We dated in the summer of sixth grade, but it wasn't serious, and we broke up after a month. But we always kept in touch and had secret feelings for each other.</p><p>We have never done anything more than talk and haven't dated since. We both get mad or awkward when the other one gets a boyfriend or girlfriend, but when it ends, we go back to each other (as friends). After my last relationship with another guy, I'm really scared to do this again. I told him that, but he keeps asking to hang out. One time, I said yes, and we just held hands and cuddled, nothing more.</p><p>I've told him again that I don't want to go into a relationship. What I want is for us to mature and wait until college, but he can still date other people. I talked to my mom about it and realized that if he got into a relationship with someone else, I'd be mad. I don't know why I can't make up my mind about a stupid boy. Can you help? — <i>LOVELORN TEEN IN NEBRASKA</i></p><p><b>DEAR TEEN:</b> You tell me you don't want to go into a relationship, but it seems to me you already have a long-standing relationship with this boy. The other relationships the two of you have are not as solid as the friendship you keep reverting to. I do agree that you should wait until you are older for either of you to have a formal relationship.</p><p>When you (both, presumably) get to college, you will meet new people and form new relationships, and your world view will broaden. I don't know what the future will bring to your relationship, but I'm pretty sure from what you have written that the friendship will be a lasting one.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I am a woman over 60, happily married with two young adult kids. Like many young adults today, they are struggling to find their way and carry a lot of emotional stress. On more than one occasion, they have blamed their father and me for their turmoil. They don't seem to know how to build their lives or to want any advice from us. It breaks my heart.</p><p>I don't know how to help them, and I don't know how to get past the feeling that somewhere along the way I may have let them down and failed as a mother to help them find their way. What can I do? — <i>MOM IN DOUBT IN PENNSYLVANIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR MOM:</b> As you stated, "Like many young adults today, they are struggling to find their way." The world has changed dramatically since you were your children's age. The expectations with which you were raised are not the realities they face in today's social and economic environment. Listen when they need to talk and let them vent. But remember, none of this is your fault. You haven't let them down. Every generation must find its own way.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Good advice for everyone — teens to seniors — is in "The Anger in All of Us and How to Deal With It." To order, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby, Anger Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Artemis II is an inspiring balm to Trump's address, reckless war
entries.summary: <p>On Wednesday evening, two historic things happened, almost simultaneously.</p><p>First, four courageous astronauts successfully lifted off from Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center aboard <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/01/chicagoan-amy-lendian-artemis-ii-launch-nasa-moon-mission" >Artemis II</a>, which will attempt the first lunar flyby in more than 50 years.</p><p>It was a stunning sight, and one that <a class="Link" href="https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1" target="_blank" >turned out</a> more than 3 million people who watched the official NASA launch broadcast .</p><p>It recalled a nostalgia for the days of Apollo, which excited and inspired so many Americans, and for just a moment, stirred up the kind of patriotism and hope that’s felt hard to come by in recent months.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-720000" name="module-720000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>In a parallel universe this would have been the perfect moment for the president to address the nation with a soaring speech marking the moment. We could have seen the kind of speech John F. Kennedy gave in 1962, inspiring an anxious nation to believe in possibility and progress amid a backdrop of Cold War fears.</p><p>But that’s not the universe or timeline we’re living in. America is at war, and we don’t really know why.</p><p>President Donald Trump <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/donald-trump-transcript-address-iran-war-b5970011fe934dde84d95d650bda56a9" target="_blank" >finally delivered his first national address</a> more than a month into his self-proclaimed — and unauthorized — “excursion” into Iran, an incursion that’s resulted in numerous U.S. casualties, strangled the free flow of oil, created turmoil in the markets and world economies, and created fear among the U.S. and our allies that our actions will have dangerous ripple effects in the region and here at home for months, possibly years.</p><p>But a speech that was meant to satisfy the concerns of a skeptical public — <a class="Link" href="https://ssrs.com/news/donald-trump-faces-deep-public-skepticism-about-iran-war-ahead-of-white-house-speech/" target="_blank" >two-thirds</a> of whom disapprove of Trump’s war and don’t believe he has a clear plan — failed to meet the mark.</p><p>Despite Trump’s rah-rah declarations of victory and decimation, no one with even a cursory knowledge of Iran’s history and capabilities could believe this reckless and aimless war of attrition is anywhere close to being over.</p><p>Offering no real plans to recover the roughly 1,000 pounds of highly enriched uranium buried in the rubble at Natanz and Isfahan, offering no real plans to liberate the people of Iran from a regime that’s thus far no different from the last one, offering no real plans to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and the world’s free flow of oil, Trump blustered, boasted and balked, all while wagging an indignant finger at our allies for not jumping into this ill-advised quagmire with us.</p><p>And in fact, in his attempt to brag about how short this war has lasted, his comparisons to other lengthy conflicts like Vietnam and the Iraq war only reminded Americans how costly and consuming this one is likely to be.</p><p>The speech also comes against the backdrop of Trump’s threats to withdraw from NATO, the post-World War II treaty that’s kept Soviet and other tyrant states from attacking the Western alliance for more than 70 years.</p><p>While Trump may not understand its importance, the world certainly does, and is collectively rebuking his reckless efforts to weaken the body that is crucial for ensuring our own national security and stability and that of Europe.</p><p>But times feel fraught, without question. A corrupt, ignorant and self-motivated wannabe dictator is prosecuting an ill-advised war using American blood and treasure, all while threatening to isolate our nation and endanger others even further. He’s wreaking economic havoc at home and across the globe, and without much care or consideration.</p><p>If we look at this mess Trump’s created too closely or for too long, it feels impossible to have hope for a better tomorrow. Perhaps that’s why now, more than ever, we need to look up and away, toward the stars, where actual heroes are leading the way. We’re all cheering you on, Artemis II.</p><p><i>S.E. Cupp is the host of “S.E. Cupp Unfiltered” on CNN.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b70000" name="module-b70000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: S. E. Cupp

entries.title: Route 66-related news is music to my ears
entries.summary: <p>Thanks to the Sun-Times for printing WBEZ's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/somer-van-benton" target="_blank" >Somer Van Benton</a>'s article on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/03/25/route-66-centennial-navy-pier-sign" >U.S. Route 66’s centennial celebration</a> and Navy Pier's new sign marking the legendary road's starting point<i> </i>connecting Chicago with Los Angeles.</p><p>While the article mentioned related historical tidbits only older readers are familiar with and Bobby Troup's famous song, it omitted mention of the song's most famous line, which is often cited as part of its title: "(Get Your Kicks on) Route 66." The song was covered by Chuck Berry and the Rolling Stones, but many of us older folks are familiar with Nat King Cole's original version that came out in 1946 — before the television age.</p><p>Last week's Route 66 celebration at Navy Pier brings back memories to us old-timers, who remember that song fondly. And what version of it is dearer to those who were around at the time than the Nat King Cole version? As many old-timers would say, "Back when music was music!"</p><p><i>Ted Z. Manuel, Hyde Park</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-980000" name="module-980000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Ivey needs Bible lessons </h3><p>Self-proclaimed Christian <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/30/league-source-says-bulls-working-exit-strategy-for-jaden-ivey" >Jaden Ivey</a> seems to have forgotten that all of us are God's children, and that regardless of whether a person is straight or gay or plays in the NBA, we are all equal in God's eyes.</p><p>Perhaps Mr. Ivey needs to spend some time contemplating <a class="Link" href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew%2022%3A39&version=NIV#:~:text=Matthew%2022:39%20NIV%20%2D%20And%20the%20second,like%20it:%20'Love%20your%20%2D%20Bible%20Gateway." target="_blank" >Matthew 22:39</a>, The Second Great Commandment of Jesus Christ: "Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thy self," and <a class="Link" href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew%207%3A3-4&version=NIV" target="_blank" >Matthew 7:3-4</a>, "Why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your neighbor's eye and pay no attention to the plank in your own eye?"</p><p><i>Chet Alexander, Alsip</i></p><h3>Ivey’s faith not problematic</h3><p>Joe Cowley's story about<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/31/jaden-ivey-fiasco-sheds-light-bulls-failing-front-office" > Jaden Ivey</a> disappointed me. A man's proclamation about Christ being in his life is not a red flag.</p><p><i>Michael Marsh, Chatham</i></p><h3>Inspired by ‘No Kings’ spirit </h3><p>On Saturday, my husband Tom and I joined our friends and neighbors in "Woke-Conda" — Wauconda — for the village's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/28/no-kings-rallies-kick-off-in-evanston-cicero-and-lincoln-square-as-hundreds-expected-to-protest-in-chicago" >“No Kings”</a> rally.</p><p>I couldn't help but think of my childhood in Rogers Park and the annual essay contest children entered titled "What Patriotism Means to Me." </p><p>At the rally, we met Beth, her best friend, her husband and their two fifth grade children who had signs that read "end war" and "I love democracy" and one that paraphrased Greta Thunberg: <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/policy/international/5342722-greta-thunberg-rips-trump-the-world-needs-a-lot-of-more-young-angry-women/" target="_blank" >“The world needs more angry women.” </a></p><p>It was uplifting to meet and witness civics lesson in action, as parents engaged with their children and instilled in them that democracy is for all Americans.</p><p>Beth's children chanted and cheered as motorists honked in support. They responded "we love you" to the few who disagreed. All good lessons.</p><p>My husband and I came home with a renewed sense of hope, knowing this family shared the real lesson of "What Patriotism Means to Me."</p><p><i>Eileen Gorrell Yacullo, retired Chicago Public Schools teacher, Wauconda</i></p><h3>Trump is a ‘mail’ man </h3><p>In response to Larry Craig, the gentleman from Wilmette who is against <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/03/30/landlords-rental-inspections-trump-tsa-dhs-immigration-loyola-capitol-riots-mail-in-ballots" target="_blank" >mail-in voting</a>. I hope you are aware that the current president has <a class="Link" href="https://www.heraldmailmedia.com/story/opinion/columns/2026/03/29/trump-vote-by-mail-florida-election/89350654007/" target="_blank" >used mail-in voting</a> recently. So if he can, WE can.</p><p><i>Daniel Olsen, Naperville</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: City program wants to bring more families to the South and West sides, one vacant lot at a time
entries.summary: <p>When Kai Bandele moved to Chicago nearly 20 years ago, it was into a two-flat in the West Side.</p><p>It was a win-win scenario: The building was owned by a friend of Bandele’s sister, and Bandele and her spouse were able to save enough money to buy a home before their son was born. </p><p>“Being able to stay in place, to save, to grow and to eventually purchase my own single-family home on the South Side — none of that would have been possible without that crutch being there for me,” Bandele said.</p><p>She's now experiencing a full-circle moment. Bandele and development partner Jim Webb were selected in February for the third round of Chicago’s Missing Middle Infill Housing program, which launched in 2024 and aims to add sorely needed market-rate homes in the South and West sides.</p><p>Their project, called Eleve West, will turn four vacant lots in West Garfield Park into four two-flats — the same type of housing Bandele first lived in. Construction is expected to start in the spring.</p><p>“My focus has been [building] single-family homes, but now, I'm able to actually step in and build homes that actually offer the exact same opportunity that I was granted,” Bandele said. “That feels really good to be able to do that for other families.”</p><p>Across Chicago, families are clambering to live in affordable, smaller-scale buildings. Two-flats and similar properties are on the market, but depending on where you look, they’re much harder to find. It's especially true in the South and West sides, where decades of disinvestment have left a patchwork of vacant lots that were once homes for middle-class families. But the city is pushing to build more of those "missing middle" homes through its program, as it looks to create more paths to homeownership without pushing existing neighbors out. </p><p>The strategy is also creating opportunities for generational wealth and economic development, developers involved in the program said.</p><p>“The work that we're doing is about bringing that layer back in a way that supports community and also creates pathways to ownership without over-building,” Bandele said. “We are not, in fact, introducing something new here. We are restoring a model that was proven to work and by providing more diverse housing supply.”</p><p>Under the program, selected developers are able to purchase vacant city-owned land for $1 and build single-family homes with accessory dwelling units, two- and three-flats, row houses, town houses or six-flats. The city also offers $150,000 in construction assistance per unit. That helps developers price the homes at an affordable rate for families making no more than 140% of the area median income, or $151,200 for a three-person household, according to the city.</p><h3>Breaking ground</h3><p>Housing has been a central part of Mayor Brandon Johnson’s platform. His $1.25 billion housing and economic development bond was approved in 2024 by the Chicago City Council, with $75 million allocated to the Missing Middle program.</p><p>The initiative has added 301 homes to the construction pipeline, according to the Chicago Department of Planning and Development. The first project under the program was in North Lawndale and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/01/06/north-lawndale-housing-development-chicago-missing-middle" target="_blank" >broke ground</a> in January.</p><p>Johnson said it’s no secret that Chicago needs “thousands and thousands” of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/06/chicago-225-000-affordable-rental-units-poorest-residents-nlihc-report" >new homes</a>, and his program is a way to build housing at scale. He projects 500 homes will be built over the next five years from the program.</p><p>“I think that the example of success will be through [neighbors’] eyes, quite frankly,” Johnson said. “Do they see the promise of the acceleration and the rejuvenation of the very communities that we're working really hard to repopulate?”</p><p>Johnson said there are 14 minority-led development teams in the program so far. He also pointed to growing interest in the program, with more than 30 applicants for its third round, as a sign of success.</p><p>The fourth round was announced Wednesday and covers lots in South Chicago and West Englewood. It includes <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/31/600-vacant-city-lots-to-hit-the-market-wednesday-including-55-for-affordable-housing" >30 parcels</a> in the two neighborhoods, which the city is pitching for town homes and two- to six-flat developments.</p><p>Department of Planning and Development Commissioner Ciere Boatright, whose department oversees the Missing Middle program, said developers recognize the “unique opportunity.” </p><p>She thinks the initiative will make it easier for developers to build these housing types without subsidies over time. She pointed to the city’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2024/06/22/lasalle-street-apartments-office-adaptive-reuse-tif-johnson" >La Salle Corridor Revitalization</a> initiative, which is giving developers subsidies to include large swaths of affordable housing when turning vacant Loop office buildings into apartments. Now, there are 20 conversion projects in the pipeline, and 14 of them don’t have any city subsidies, according to Boatright.</p><p>“You're making a market that exists a stronger market,” she said.</p><h3>What’s missing</h3><p>Middle income housing is also a statewide issue. Gov. JB Pritzker’s office formed a temporary committee in 2024 to explore solutions for the lack of missing middle housing.</p><p>While Illinois has implemented tactics to boost the affordable housing stock, the committee didn't find a similar effort toward expanding housing options for those making between 80% and 140% of the area median income. An individual at 80% in Chicago would have an annual income of $67,150, while 140% of the area median income would be $117,600.</p><p>The committee produced a suite of solutions aimed at increasing development and lowering costs. Among the proposals was a missing middle homeownership program, offering subsidies to homebuyers.</p><p>Pritzker’s recent Building Up Illinois Developments plan, dubbed BUILD, proposes solutions to cut the administrative red tape in housing, along with strategies to increase supply and lower costs, as well as $100 million in capital funding to support middle housing construction. The funds would be administered by the Illinois Housing Development Authority. BUILD would also legalize a wider range of housing types statewide, such as duplexes and four-flats.</p><p>Bob Palmer, policy director at Housing Action Illinois, said missing middle housing keeps the market balanced. If there’s a shortage of missing middle housing, those individuals will instead live in affordable housing, reducing the housing options for the state’s most vulnerable.</p><p>Webb said Eleve West wouldn't be possible without Chicago's Missing Middle program. He said each building would sell for at least $300,000 if not for the construction subsidy the city provides through the program. The developers have yet to determine their units' sale price. They're going through environmental testing on the site, which may affect its final listing prices.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8873f27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fae%2F58%2F94d62feb4b0fb3babbb5a5ec49d9%2Fhudson.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6ace458/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fae%2F58%2F94d62feb4b0fb3babbb5a5ec49d9%2Fhudson.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rendering of Eleve West</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by TRU Bandele and Guider Group</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>High construction and labor costs are some of the barriers developers deal with when trying to build missing middle housing. Eleve West, on West Jackson Boulevard, is a $3.1 million investment.</p><p>“It did not work on paper without us displacing people,” said Webb, who’s also managing partner at TruDelta.</p><p>Boatright said the Missing Middle program is about recognizing the importance of housing diversity.</p><p>“We have high-rises in Fulton Market. We've got traditional single-family homes being developed on vacant lots,” she said. “This is an opportunity to … really promote density and address infill housing in a unique way.”</p><p>The program is also a way to address population loss and the city’s glut of vacant land. As of spring 2025, the city owns more than 7,000 empty residential lots. Many of them are clustered in neighborhoods whose populations are “substantially less than 50% of their 1960 levels,” according to the city.</p><p>“This strategic decision to invest in housing — particularly missing middle — it has to address all facets of the predicaments and challenges that people in Chicago have endured,” Johnson said. “We have to build more units. They have to be affordable and we have to create a pathway to long-term sustainability. That is the essence of what the missing middle initiative is about.”</p><h3>Expanding the program</h3><p>Boatright said aldermen and community members have been asking when the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/05/26/chicago-missing-middle-housing-program-developer-deadline" >Missing Middle program</a> will come to their neighborhood.</p><p>Abraham Lacy was among them. The president of Far South Community Development Corp. was looking forward to the program coming to Morgan Park and other South Side neighborhoods, after it launched on the West Side.</p><p>Far South CDC was announced as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/08/26/developers-missing-middle-housing-south-side" >one of the developers</a> in August 2025, during the program’s second round.</p><p>Lacy said it's building four three-flats, at 10726-34 S. Loomis St. and 10717 S. Glenroy Ave., that will be sold as condos. </p><p>Compared to neighborhoods like Hyde Park, residents in Morgan Park can transition from renting to owning a home without leaving the neighborhood, he said. </p><p>He said the Missing Middle program will help Morgan Park boost its population because more housing options will be available to those priced out of other Chicago neighborhoods.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-980000" name="image-980000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium">
