href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-m/" target="_blank" >Local officials warn of ICE arrests at domestic violence courthouse</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-q/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p><b>Accusations against feds</b>: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents are violating Illinois law and making arrests at Chicago-area courthouses, local officials alleged at a press conference Thursday. The Cook County Public Defender’s office said since late February, agents have been spotted entering the downtown Domestic Violence Courthouse or waiting directly outside. In at least two instances, someone was arrested. Agents had returned to the courthouse Thursday.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Gov. JB Pritzker signed a law in December banning federal agents from conducting civil immigration enforcement at state courthouses after the chief judge’s office barred civil arrests at county courthouses in October. Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle also signed an executive order prohibiting the feds from staging on county property as Chicago, Evanston and others did the same. The law doesn't carry criminal penalties; it requires a person, if detained, to file a civil lawsuit.</p><p><b>More on immigration</b>:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-b7b5e382-2f61-11f1-bd48-95283f4c77e1"><li>Chicago police Supt. Larry Snelling on Thursday <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-a/" target="_blank" >joined the Community Commission for Public Safety and Accountability for a “special hearing” to discuss his department’s interactions with federal immigration agents</a>. Some of his comments prompted boos from the audience.<br> </li><li>Cook County’s top prosecutor <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-f/" target="_blank" >abruptly dropped a felony case against a DePaul University student accused of spitting on an ICE agent</a> over the weekend at O’Hare Airport.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-z/" target="_blank" >READ MORE</a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-ti/" target="_blank" >Marshall High School boys basketball team’s state championship brings pride to West Side</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-td/" target="_blank" >Cindy Hernandez</a></h5><p>When the boys’ basketball team at John Marshall Metropolitan High School <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-th/" target="_blank" >took home the Class 1A state title</a> last month, it was bigger than just one school.</p><p>"This championship wasn’t just for us, it was for the West Side of Chicago," head coach Darrin Laye said Thursday, when the squad was honored with a celebration at the school.</p><p>Laye credited the victory, the school’s first basketball championship since 2008, to the players’ hard work and dedication. Marshall won the 3A title in 2008 and also captured state championships in 1958 and 1960.</p><p>"These kids deserve it," Laye said.</p><p>Students, faculty, parents and even alumni packed the auditorium of the East Garfield Park school, welcoming the team with applause and cheers.</p><p>"When that buzzer rang it was a great feeling," senior and team member Quinton Gibson said before the celebration. Gibson said his teammates kept each other motivated to continue to put in the work to succeed.</p><p>"We’re a family," Gibson said. "We all got each other. They’re like my brothers."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wirldo-l-tk/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>Do you live near a vacant lot? What would you like to see done with it?</b> </p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Maintenance worker shot in lobby of Kenwood apartment building dies
entries.summary: <p>A 42-year-old maintenance worker who was shot Thursday evening during an argument with a resident of a Kenwood apartment complex has died, officials say. </p><p>Police responded about 5:35 p.m. to a person shot at the building at 4554 S. Drexel Blvd. and found the victim in the lobby, shot in his left hand, right shoulder and chest, according to a police report. </p><p>Officers spoke to a witness who said after a resident of the building became embroiled in an argument with the victim, the resident returned to the lobby of the building and opened fire, hitting the 42-year-old man, the report said. </p><p>He was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center initially in serious condition but died there at 8:06 p.m., according to the Cook County medical examiner's office. </p><p>The shooter fled and was not in custody. </p><p>Area 1 detectives are investigating. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Motorist fatally shoots self after crashing into several vehicles including PACE bus in Oak Park: police
entries.summary: <p>A motorist who blew a red light and crashed into three other vehicles including a PACE bus injuring 14 people shot themselves to death Friday morning in Oak Park, according to police.</p><p>The adult motorist was behind the wheel of an SUV with out of state plates and was southbound on Harlem Avenue when they drove through a red light at Lake Street about 9 a.m., according to Dan Yopchick, a police spokesperson. </p><p>A pedestrian was in the crosswalk at that moment, and the driver swerved out of the way and into the northbound lanes, where they crashed into three other vehicles including a PACE bus, according to Yopchick. </p><p>“The driver of the initial SUV then took out a gun and shot themselves in the head,” Yopchick said.</p><p>The person, who Yopchick only described as an adult who Oak Park police have no history with, was pronounced dead on the scene inside the crashed SUV by Oak Park fire department paramedics. </p><p>No one else was injured by gunfire, but about 14 other people, primarily from the PACE bus, were injured in the crash and taken to area hospitals. None suffered serious injuries, according to Yopchick.</p><p>A motive for the incident was not immediately known, though Yopchick said they were not being followed by law enforcement at the time.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Your guide to EXPO Chicago 2026
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s largest art event, EXPO Chicago, is returning to Navy Pier April 9-12. And while this year’s fair is smaller, its new director, Kate Sierzputowski, says the event is going to feel “a little bit more intentional.”</p><p>In the mix are 130 galleries from at least two dozen countries, down from last year’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts/2025/04/17/expo-chicago-guide-art-galleries-navy-pier" target="_blank" ><u>nearly 200 featured galleries</u></a>. This year’s fair is the third iteration since global arts conglomerate <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/7/14/23795354/expo-chicago-acquired-by-international-art-show-producer-frieze" target="_blank" ><u>Frieze added EXPO to its portfolio in 2023</u></a>.</p><div target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>Many local gallerists and art collectors said 2026 feels like the year attendees will truly feel the “Frieze effect” on the art fair. For starters, it has new leadership. Sierzputowski is successor to EXPO Chicago founder Tony Karman, who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/05/29/expo-chicago-founder-tony-karman-stepping-down-from-director-role" target="_blank" ><u>stepped down from his role</u></a> as director last May. He founded the contemporary and modern art fair in 2012 and launched it as a descendant of Art Chicago, a fair that ran from 1980 to 2011.</p><p>The floor has also been “refined,” said Sierzputowski. The fair made a “controversial” decision to cut out booths from not-for-profit groups this year, resulting in fewer exhibitors, explains longtime Chicago gallerist and art dealer Monique Meloche, who sits on EXPO’s selection committee. That means that groups like the <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoartistscoalition.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Artists Coalition</u></a> aren’t exhibiting this year.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a20000" name="image-a20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a1aca2f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F2e%2F10116e864c53ae65ad189e574e0c%2Fexpoguide-260405-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/199e45c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F2e%2F10116e864c53ae65ad189e574e0c%2Fexpoguide-260405-6.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/939523a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F2e%2F10116e864c53ae65ad189e574e0c%2Fexpoguide-260405-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Kate Sierzputowski, director of EXPO Chicago, sits near the EXPO Chicago entrance at Navy Pier, Friday, March 27, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/939523a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F2e%2F10116e864c53ae65ad189e574e0c%2Fexpoguide-260405-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c3960a3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F2e%2F10116e864c53ae65ad189e574e0c%2Fexpoguide-260405-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/939523a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F2e%2F10116e864c53ae65ad189e574e0c%2Fexpoguide-260405-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Director Kate Sierzputowski is successor to EXPO Chicago founder Tony Karman, who stepped down from his role as director last May.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Some insiders hope the new floorplan and fewer galleries will result in more engagement between audiences and galleries and artists, representing such art-forward cities as Paris, London and Tokyo, but also Seoul and Busan in South Korea; Lagos, Nigeria; Taipei, Taiwan and Nassau, Bahamas.</p><p>So far, disruptions in international artist travel due to visa issues haven’t surfaced, said EXPO Chicago leadership. But art has always responded to the moment, said EXPO Chicago’s curator Essence Harden.</p><p>“I don't know if it's resolved by the fair,” Harden said. “I don't think it is, but it does allow for, hopefully, another way of relating and being with each other. It's still art and people still make it and it's still one of the most essential human qualities of our species.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6f0000" name="module-6f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>Planning for EXPO Chicago</b></h3><br><ul id="rte-e4b074f2-2ed4-11f1-b481-45c942df77c9"><li><b>Tickets: </b>Admission for the opening night on Thursday, April 9 is ticketed and<a class="Link" href="https://www.expochicago.com/tickets" target="_blank" ><u> $165 per person</u></a>. Single-day admission tickets start at $40, and three-day admission tickets start at $68. Guided tours can be booked for $30, and seniors and students may receive discounts. Children under 12 get in for free.</li><li><b>Parking:</b> Know that there are <a class="Link" href="https://navypier.org/plan-your-visit/getting-here-and-parking/parking-and-valet/" target="_blank" ><u>limited parking spots</u></a> at Navy Pier if you plan to drive in. Parking is available on a first-come, first-served basis in the East and West garages. If you decide to catch a ride through Uber, Lyft, or other ride-sharing services, tell the driver you are looking for “Entrance 2.”</li><li><b>Public transit:</b> Five different buses can drop you off at Navy Pier: the 29, 65, 6, 124 and 2 (the latter is only available during weekday mornings and evenings). <a class="Link" href="https://www.expochicago.com/visit/getting-to-navy-pier" target="_blank" ><u>Here is more information on getting to Navy Pier.</u></a></li></ul></div> </div><p></p><h3>What to expect from EXPO Chicago 2026</h3><h3></h3><p>As in previous years, EXPO Chicago will set up inside Navy Pier’s Festival Hall through curated sections. “Focus” highlights emerging artists. “Profile” features projects from international galleries like <a class="Link" href="https://47canal.us/" target="_blank" ><u>47 Canal</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.materia-art.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Matéria</u></a>.</p><p>At EXPO itself, expect a good mix of blue-chip galleries and mid-career artist galleries, as well as a solid Chicago footprint.The talk series <a class="Link" href="https://www.expochicago.com/programs/dialogues" target="_blank" ><u>Dialogues</u></a> is back again. One highlight will be an April 10 panel featuring SAIC fashion professor <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2026/02/12/nick-cave-artist-mammoths-soundsuits-sculpture-smithsonian" target="_blank" ><u>Nick Cave,</u></a> whose work is currently on exhibition at the Smithsonian. Artists Devin T. Mays and Miriam Stoney join a discussion moderated by Gertie's director of curatorial affairs, Garteth Thomas Kaye, on April 12 on Chicago’s connection to Vienna, Austria. Spoiler: It’s not just hot dogs that came about as a result of collaboration between the two.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>On April 9, <a class="Link" href="https://www.antonkerngallery.com/artists/111-aliza-nisenbaum/" target="_blank" ><u>Aliza Nisenbaum</u></a>, who was commissioned to work on a <a class="Link" href="https://viewingroom.regenprojects.com/viewing-room/aliza-nisenbaum-at-expo-chicago-2026-online-viewing-room#tab:thumbnails" target="_blank" ><u>mural for the Obama Presidential Center</u></a>, will appear on a panel alongside other artists tapped to create work for the new center.</p><p>About a dozen Chicago galleries will be featured, too. Document in West Town will showcase <a class="Link" href="https://privateviews.artlogic.net/2/5270ae38186cc300f9fc48/" target="_blank" ><u>works from local and international artists</u></a>, from a dye sublimation print by French-Caribbean artist <a class="Link" href="https://www.juliencreuzet.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Julien Creuzet</u></a>, to an archival pigment print, “Coming to the Commons 4,” by University of Chicago professor Laura Letinsky.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.richardgraygallery.com/" target="_blank" ><u>GRAY Chicago</u></a> will bring work from the artist <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts/2024/11/27/torkwase-dyson-gray-gallery-chicago-south-shore-new-art-show" target="_blank" ><u>Torkwase Dyson</u></a> to the EXPO Chicago floor, said Valerie Carberry, GRAY’s president and CEO. The gallery will also be featured in a special section curated by the Obama Presidential Center.</p><h3><b>What’s new this year?</b></h3><p>Even though it does not open to the public until Juneteenth, the Obama Presidential Center will offer a preview of its collection through “Evolution” and “Embodiment,” two exhibitions curated by Dr. Louise Bernard, founding director of the center and the Obama Foundation’s vice president.</p><p>Materials will be sourced from the soon-to-be-open museum’s art commissions, alongside models of the presidential center. Bernard said partnering with EXPO helps bridge the gap between the construction of the campus and the forward-looking vision behind it.</p><p>“We really think about that idea of humanism and the role of empathy and how we are all connected,” Bernard said.</p><p>The Obama Center display includes a collaboration between Regen Projects and Anton Kern Gallery, based in Los Angeles and New York City, that will feature <a class="Link" href="https://viewingroom.regenprojects.com/viewing-room/aliza-nisenbaum-at-expo-chicago-2026-online-viewing-room#tab:thumbnails" target="_blank" ><u>13 of Nisenbaum’s oil and watercolor paintings</u></a> to warm audiences up for her big mural reveal.</p><p>“They're studies or different things that relate to the mural that [Nisenbaum] made,” said Shaun Regen, of Regen Projects. “It's like a love letter to learning, to reading, to literature, to the humanities and also to Chicago.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div EXPO will offer plenty of works on offer. Here are names of local artists to watch that popped up in interviews with exhibiting gallerists and local art collectors.</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-89a2ddb0-2eb0-11f1-bc98-a30bea4a21ca"><li><a class="Link" href="https://www.faheemmajeed.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Faheem Majeed</u></a> is a Chicago-based sculpture and installation artist with deep ties to the South Side. His work has been displayed at the Museum of Contemporary Art and is a co-director of the <a class="Link" href="https://floatingmuseum.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Floating Musuem</u></a>.</li><li><a class="Link" href="https://www.yvettemayorga.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Yvette Mayorga</u></a> is a young, Mexican-American artist who specializes in a style she calls “Latinx-oco,” in homage to first-generation life. Much of her work is playful and bright pink, and she holds a degree in painting and anthropology from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. Her work will be on display at Meloche’s booth.</li><li><a class="Link" href="https://www.pedromontilla.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Pedro Montilla</u></a> is a painter from Bogotá, Colombia, who currently lectures at SAIC. He usually creates tapestries by painting on fique fabric (a material used to transport coffee and produce), connecting his Andean heritage with modern techniques.</li><li><a class="Link" href="https://lukeagada.com/?utm_source=ig&utm_medium=social&utm_content=link_in_bio&fbclid=PAZXh0bgNhZW0CMTEAc3J0YwZhcHBfaWQMMjU2MjgxMDQwNTU4AAGnlPrGEE9BGNnyRYsRiHen4_sutChuPpKpM4zLXdovgfpoq1FyW2DLXElTaPI_aem_TVR53hbZ6B4A5qLGVXkeGQ" target="_blank" ><u>Luke Agada</u></a> is a painter and drawer from Lagos, Nigeria, who graduated from SAIC in 2023. His work has been exhibited worldwide, and he was named Newcity’s Breakout Artist of 2024.</li><li><a class="Link" href="https://lauraletinsky.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Laura Letinsky</u></a> is a Canadian-born photographer and sculptor best known for her decaying still lifes. She is a professor at the University of Chicago’s visual arts department.<br></li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Laura Letinsky is a Canadian-born photographer and sculptor best known for her decaying still lifes. Her work will be on display at EXPO.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Laura Letinsky</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Tips for aspiring art collectors</b></h3><p>Publicist Beth Heller and her husband have been collecting art together for more than 25 years. She said aspiring art collectors should look out for galleries that offer payment plans for artwork, to avoid fronting the full price of work.</p><p>Aron Gent, of Document, said that attendees shouldn’t stop at EXPO: If they find a gallery or artist they like at the art fair, he encourages them to visit the gallery for themselves.“A lot of people will come to town from the suburbs or surrounding area and go to EXPO … but not really go and explore,” Gent said. “You really need to invest in your curiosity with contemporary art and go to these physical brick-and-mortar spaces.”</p><p>Meloche recommends checking out the “Focus” section first, where typical exhibitions include art from younger, up-and-coming artists and galleries at more attainable prices.</p><p>Tommy Reyes, owner of Gallery19 and long-time EXPO attendee, recommends looking for galleries selling prints of artwork that are usually cheaper than buying the real thing.</p><p>“Don't think of it as an investment,” Reyes adds. “Think: Do you love it? If there is a connection, then that piece is meant for you.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div </div></div><p></p><h3><b>Beyond EXPO Chicago </b></h3><p>Plenty happens in Chicago outside of Navy Pier in the weeks ahead.</p><p>Seven minutes from Navy Pier, a new art fair called<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://www.neighbors.art/" target="_blank" ><u>Neighbors</u></a> is operating as a satellite to EXPO from April 8 to 12. Founded by arts patron Mirka Serrato, the fair will take place inside a historic apartment in the Gold Coast. London’s Harleson High Street and Chicago’s Shanghai Seminary are participating.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.barelyfair.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Barely Fair,</u></a> an artist-run fair happening in McKinley Park, kicks off April 3 and runs through the 19th. The fair will present 24 global and local exhibitors, including Galerie Noah Klink from Berlin, Ortega y Gasset Projects in Brooklyn, and Chicago’s own Hans Goodrich.</p><p>Citywide, several ancillary events and installations will pop up, including “OVERRIDE,” which will feature artworks on digital billboards and informational panels on bus stops and street signs through April 19. Featured Chicagoans include Aimée Beaubien and Hattie Lee Mendoza.</p><p>For fairgoers looking for opportunities to mix and mingle in neighborhoods, South Side Night is set to take place in Hyde Park, Bronzeville and the nearby areas on April 7, while Art After Hours on April 10 features extended hours and programming at several exhibiting Chicago galleries. Some, such as <a class="Link" href="https://www.lesenluminures.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Les Enluminures</u></a>, are hosting a show on rare Medieval manuscripts at their Downtown space.</p><p>And in the West Loop, Intuit Art Museum is hosting a dance party on April 10 at City Hall Events (838 W. Kinzie St.). Tickets are $75 per person.</p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Park West Chicago music venue founder Dale Niedermaier has died at 89
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p>Dale Niedermaier was one of those guys who did what he wanted.</p><p>In 1977, Mr. Niedermaier became the primary investor in a group that gutted an old movie theater in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lincoln-park" >Lincoln Park</a> to create one of Chicago's most intimate music venues: Park West, 322 W. Armitage Ave.</p><p>Under Mr. Niedermaier's ownership through the late 1980s, Park West hosted concerts by performers including Prince, Bob Dylan, U2, Tina Turner, Aretha Franklin and Stevie Wonder. The Bears also recorded the “Super Bowl Shuffle” there in 1985.</p><p>Before getting into the music industry, Mr. Niedermaier and his wife Judy Niedermaier ran Niedermaier, Inc., which designed product displays for shops and department stores including Henri Bendel, Bergdorf Goodman, Bloomingdale’s, Gucci, Macy’s, Marshall Field’s, Nike, Ralph Lauren and Tiffany.</p><p>The theater had several lives before becoming <a class="Link" href="https://www.jamusa.com/venues/park-west-history" target="_blank" >Park West.</a> Its immediate predecessor was the Town Theater, a burlesque and pornographic movie house that was shut down by the city in 1973 to halt the showing of "Deep Throat."</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier brought an eye for design to the theater's facelift. It became known for its tiered booth interior, small dance floor, "Star Wars"-style marquee and post-modern look that incorporated the aluminum veneer alucabon.</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier partnered with Chicago's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/3/11/22969374/jam-productions-jerry-mickelson-arny-granat-anniversary-50th-music-promotion" >Jam Productions</a> to book talent for shows.</p><p>"Dale was a larger-than-life guy, and he lived life on his own terms," Jam founder Jerry Mickelson said. "He did whatever he wanted to do, and he did it well, in his own authentic way. And everybody really loved the guy."</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier raised his kids in Winnetka but was separated from his wife and living in an apartment across the street from his music venue when he got a call from Mickelson in 1978. Mickelson told him the Rolling Stones were coming to Chicago and wanted to avoid the city's commotion by staying in a quiet suburb. </p><p>Mr. Niedermaier asked his wife to host them in Winnetka. She agreed, with some hesitation, and Mr. Niedermaier sent a Park West worker to fully stock the bar at the house, which sat on an acre lot and had a swimming pool.</p><p>"They stayed up all night playing music and doing whatever," said the couple's son Jeffrey Niedermaier. "My mother just rolled her eyes. She wasn't starstruck by any means."</p><p>"They'd stay at our house every time they were in town after that," Diana Hayden, the Niedermaiers' daughter, said of the rock stars. "Those were fun times."</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier died March 22 from natural causes in Austin, Texas, according to his family. He was 89.</p><p>He was born in Chicago on March 7, 1937. Family lore has it that Mr. Niedermaier was born on a table in a tavern near Fullerton and Clybourn where his mother Genevieve was a cook and his father George, who held several jobs, often could be found on a barstool doing a crossword puzzle.</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier went to Lake View High School, where he played on the football team.</p><p>While attending the University of Miami, Mr. Niedermaier worked days as a cabana boy at a Key Biscayne hotel, delivered flowers, worked construction and took night classes in business and mortgage banking.</p><p>After college, he went into mortgage banking in Miami before joining the Army. He later moved back to Chicago, befriended and partnered with a Bohemian artist who printed silkscreen banners. He opened a shop on Lincoln Avenue, hiring students from the School of the Art Institute. But soon he transitioned into creating elaborate holiday window displays and product promotions.</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier loved to sail and competed in the Chicago Yacht Club Race to Mackinac. His boat was named Park West and had the venue's logo emblazoned on one of its sails. He was a marathon runner and triathlete. He loved muscle cars and luxury automobiles and toured the country on a BMW motorcycle that he rode as far south as Panama.</p><p>"He picked me up at summer camp one year on his motorcycle, and the camp didn't want to give me to him at first because he looked like a lunatic, but they did, and I rode on the back as we traveled through Death Valley," Jeffrey Niedermaeir said. "The wind was like a blow-dryer on my face all the way to Los Angeles. We stayed in tents the whole way, and, when we arrived in L.A., we checked in to the Beverly Wilshire."</p><p>Mr. Niedermaier reunited with his former wife and cared for her toward the end of her life. She died of cancer in 2011.</p><p>In addition to his son and daughter, he is survived by four grandchildren and one great-grandchild. One of his grandchildren is former Blackhawks player John Hayden. </p><p>A celebration of life is being planned.<br></p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Advierten sobre arrestos de ICE en corte de violencia doméstica, afirman que agentes están violando la ley
entries.summary: <p>Los agentes federales de inmigración están violando la ley de Illinois y realizando arrestos en las cortes del área de Chicago, afirmaron varios funcionarios locales en una conferencia de prensa el jueves.</p><p>La oficina del Defensor Público del Condado de Cook dijo que desde finales de febrero, se ha visto a agentes federales de inmigración ingresar a la Corte de Violencia Doméstica en el 555 W. Harrison St., o esperando directamente afuera.</p><p>En al menos dos de esos casos, alguien fue arrestado, incluida una mujer que fue <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/groups/378158360786470/posts/1530031978932430/" target="_blank" >seguida fuera de la corte</a>y detenida por agentes federales el 10 de marzo. El otro incidente ocurrió el 27 de febrero, dijeron los funcionarios. Otra persona fue detenida el 18 de marzo en un lugar diferente, en la corte de Maywood.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-450000" name="module-450000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La conferencia de prensa se llevó a cabo horas después de que los agentes federales regresaran a la corte con “la intención de tomar custodia de una persona que estaba programada para comparecer en una audiencia hoy”, dijo la oficina del Defensor Público del Condado de Cook.</p><p>Los funcionarios señalaron que no estaban al tanto de que se hicieran arrestos el jueves, pero destacaron que no siempre saben de inmediato cuando alguien es detenido.</p><p>“Las sobrevivientes deberían poder entrar a una corte sin miedo a que hacer lo correcto les ponga un blanco en la espalda”, dijo la senadora de Illinois Graciela Guzmán, demócrata por Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/ICIRR/videos/1656661488801326" target="_blank" >en la conferencia de prensa del jueves</a>. “Esto es un desprecio por nuestras leyes, nuestras cortes y las sobrevivientes”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-faea0a" name="image-faea0a"></a> Clark St. en el Loop, el jueves." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/14c5806/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d4ff143/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/14c5806/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2Fe2%2F89870f11464797e4fab006a26450%2Ficepresser-260403-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>La senadora de Illinois, Graciela Guzmán, demócrata por Chicago, habla sobre la reciente ejecución de inmigración durante una conferencia de prensa en las oficinas del Condado de Cook, ubicadas en 118 S. Clark St. en el Loop, el jueves.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) y el Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) no respondieron de inmediato a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker firmó una ley en diciembre que prohíbe a los agentes federales llevar a cabo la aplicación de la ley civil de inmigración en las cortes estatales después de que la oficina del Juez Principal <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/16/juez-principal-del-condado-de-cook-prohibe-arrestos-civiles-en-las-cortes" target="_blank" >prohibiera los arrestos civiles en las cortes del condado</a> en octubre. La presidenta de la Junta del Condado de Cook, Toni Preckwinkle, también firmó una orden ejecutiva <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/17/preckwinkle-emite-orden-ejecutiva-para-crear-zonas-libres-de-ice-en-el-condado-de-cook" target="_blank" >prohibiendo a los federales realizar operativos en las propiedades del condado</a>, como <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/07/alcalde-johnson-firma-una-orden-ejecutiva-declarando-propiedades-de-la-ciudad-como-zonas-libres-de-ice" target="_blank" >hicieron Chicago</a>, Evanston y otros.</p><p>Los funcionarios señalaron un cambio en la actividad de inmigración federal, alejándose de los vecindarios donde <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/13/agentes-federales-usan-gas-lacrimogeno-en-albany-park-otro-vecindario-de-chicago" target="_blank" >enfrentaron una fuerte resistencia</a>, y hacia lugares como cortes y <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/24/despliegan-a-agentes-de-ice-en-ohare-mientras-continua-el-cierre-parcial-del-gobierno-que-afecta-a-viajeros" target="_blank" >aeropuertos</a> donde puede ser más difícil para las comunidades responder.</p><p>Sharlyn Grace, defensora pública adjunta de políticas en la oficina del Defensor Público del Condado de Cook, dijo que los informes de agentes federales utilizando las propiedades del condado para hacer arrestos habían disminuido desde la orden de Preckwinkle que les prohibía hacerlo, lo que vio como una buena señal de las herramientas legislativas que los funcionarios sí tenían.</p><p>Sin embargo, la ley que alegan que los agentes federales están violando no conlleva penas criminales; exige que la persona detenida presente una demanda civil, algo que ella admitió que ya es un desafío para aquellos detenidos por los federales.</p><p>“Es una de nuestras únicas herramientas”, dijo Grace. “Pero está obviamente limitada para que una persona en custodia de inmigración acceda a un abogado”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure Ella y otros funcionarios locales llevaron a cabo una conferencia de prensa en el Loop el jueves.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Maralea Negrón, vicepresidenta de políticas y impacto en The Network: Advocacy Against Domestic Violence, dijo que la presencia de agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza y de ICE en las cortes tendría un “efecto paralizante” en las sobrevivientes de violencia doméstica que trabajan con el sistema de justicia.</p><p>“Nuestra sociedad les dice a las sobrevivientes que informen a las fuerzas del orden y vayan a las cortes, y sin embargo, cuando lo hacen, ellas y sus familias pueden ser detenidas”, dijo Negrón. “La presencia de ICE hace que la decisión de dejar una relación o un hogar abusivo sea aún más difícil”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: NFL rejects Bears' appeal to receive compensatory draft picks for Falcons hiring Ian Cunningham
entries.summary: <p>The NFL officially rejected <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a>’ appeal to receive compensatory draft picks related to the Falcons hiring former executive Ian Cunningham as their general manager, saying Friday it informed the organization the matter is closed.</p><p>The Bears believed they were entitled to a third-round pick this year and next as part of a rule that rewards teams for developing minority coaches and executives that land jobs as head coaches or general managers. Cunningham, who is Black, was their assistant general manager for four years under Ryan Poles.</p><p>The Falcons, however, hired former quarterback Matt Ryan as their President of Football, choosing him over Cunningham for that position, and the NFL ruled that Ryan is the “Primary Football Executive,” for which the policy was designed.</p><p>“The league determined Mr. Cunningham did not fill that role with the Falcons as it is defined in league rules,” a spokesperson said in a statement.</p><p>Speaking on behalf of the Falcons in February, Cunningham said he believed the Bears should receive the draft picks. Ryan, who has zero personnel experience, said in an interview with CBS after hiring Cunningham as general manager, “I’ve got a lot to learn... Ian is in charge and he is driving this boat.”</p><p>Bears owner George McCaskey, president Kevin Warren and Poles <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/01/bears-still-hope-for-compensatory-picks-after-meeting-with-roger-goodell-about-ian-cunningham-situation" >flew to New York recently to make their case to commissioner Roger Goodell</a>. That included presenting Ryan’s comments and other evidence.</p><p>"We did what the league wants every member club to do,” McCaskey said Wednesday. “We identified diverse talent, we recruited him, we created a position for him... We allowed him to make mistakes and to learn from those mistakes. We gave him supervisory duties. We gave him training.</p><p>“We made him ready to be a general manager in the NFL, and he's getting his opportunity and we're thrilled for him."</p><p>Here is the full statement from the NFL:</p><p>"The matter is now closed following the club’s appeal. The NFL informed the Bears today they will not receive compensatory picks. The policy is designed to provide picks for the Primary Football Executive position. The League determined Mr. Cunningham did not fill that role with the Falcons as it is defined in League rules."</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Devotees commemorate 49th annual 'Via Crucis' in Pilsen
entries.summary: <p>Hundreds marched alongside a bloodied man carrying a wooden cross down West 18th Street in Pilsen on Friday morning.</p><p>The man, wearing a crown of thorns and white sheet, was dressed as Jesus Christ for the 49th annual “Via Crucis: Living Way of the Cross,” a Good Friday tradition that commemorates his passion and life. </p><p>The procession retraces the journey of Jesus Christ leading to his crucifixion. It regularly draws hundreds of Catholic devotees who come together to honor and uphold the traditions of their faith.</p><p>"This weekend is a time for reflection and a time of renewing promises," said Ivy Blanco of Stickney.</p><p>Blanco said attending the procession is a yearly tradition for her family, but it felt different this year. She said her father, who died last year, was noticeably absent from Friday’s ceremony.</p><p>"This is something that he really enjoyed," said Blanco. "He worked for the Catholic church for many years in Puerto Rico so it was important for me to continue to attend." </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" entries.author: Candace Dane Chambers

entries.title: Chicago murals: Traveling artists reach for books, not bans, in Humboldt Park
entries.summary: <p>At a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/humboldt-park" >Humboldt Park</a> building he owns, Hubie Greenwald chose to illustrate an issue that fires him up: banned books, specifically bans recently pushed through by some conservative Republicans.</p><p>“Our job is to educate our kids, and hopefully they’ll make a better world,” says Greenwald, president of Chicago-based Greenwald Co. real estate firm. “Why would you deny any human the ability to read something, even if you don’t agree with it?”</p><p>The Chicago-based American Library Association <a class="Link" href="https://www.ala.org/news/2025/04/american-library-association-kicks-national-library-week-top-10-most-challenged-books" target="_blank" >reported last year</a> that 72% of demands to censor books are now coming from organized groups. While Illinois made it more difficult to book bans in 2024, surrounding <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/race-class-communities/2023/08/31/illinois-is-a-haven-against-book-bans-in-midwest" >Midwest states have tried to make it easier</a>. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-da0000" name="image-da0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/029fde8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6258x3512+0+330/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F53%2F5abfe0e548538c129dfbdbb97573%2Fmurals-cats-260405-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d802ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6258x3512+0+330/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F53%2F5abfe0e548538c129dfbdbb97573%2Fmurals-cats-260405-2.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f609476/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6258x3512+0+330/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F53%2F5abfe0e548538c129dfbdbb97573%2Fmurals-cats-260405-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="MURALS-CATS_260405_2.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f609476/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6258x3512+0+330/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F53%2F5abfe0e548538c129dfbdbb97573%2Fmurals-cats-260405-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f9f48be/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6258x3512+0+330/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F53%2F5abfe0e548538c129dfbdbb97573%2Fmurals-cats-260405-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f609476/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6258x3512+0+330/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F53%2F5abfe0e548538c129dfbdbb97573%2Fmurals-cats-260405-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Hubie Greenwald, owner of the building at 2950 W. Chicago Ave. in Humboldt Park where Menace and Resa painted a mural, stands in front of the building, Wednesday, March 25, 2026. | Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>So Greenwald hired the New York artists known as Menace and Resa in early 2025 to paint a tall, narrow mural of children reaching for books above and surrounding the doorway to his building at 2950 W. Chicago Ave., home of Humboldt Park Montessori. Book titles include Margaret Atwood’s "The Handmaid’s Tale," "Forever" by Judy Blume and "1984" by George Orwell on the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ala.org/bbooks/frequentlychallengedbooks/top100" target="_blank" >American Library Association’s lists of frequently banned books</a>.</p><p>While Greenwald suggested the concept, “we were very much behind this one,” Menace says. “Knowledge should never be banned.”</p><p>Menace and Resa have added art to Chicago walls before. Resa is from Chicago, and the two travel the country in their RV with a goal of painting murals in all 50 states. <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/menaceresa/" target="_blank" >So far they’ve hit 20</a>. They <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/murals-mosaics/2022/1/14/22865900/chicago-murals-mural-mafia-graffiti" target="_blank" >visited Chicago in 2022 with their four-person group, Mural Mafia</a>, adding murals of singer and Chicago native Jennifer Hudson along with Malcolm X, Rosa Parks and the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr., among others. One of those was on the back of Greenwald's Humboldt Park building.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-880000" name="image-880000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bd9d312/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fc8%2F7e6ab31b420592eb8e701a98a505%2Fmurals-cats-260405-9.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d579c42/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fc8%2F7e6ab31b420592eb8e701a98a505%2Fmurals-cats-260405-9.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ea1293c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fc8%2F7e6ab31b420592eb8e701a98a505%2Fmurals-cats-260405-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A mural shows Chicago native Jennifer Hudson along with Malcolm X, Rosa Parks and the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. on the back of a building." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ea1293c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fc8%2F7e6ab31b420592eb8e701a98a505%2Fmurals-cats-260405-9.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d4cea32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fc8%2F7e6ab31b420592eb8e701a98a505%2Fmurals-cats-260405-9.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ea1293c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fc8%2F7e6ab31b420592eb8e701a98a505%2Fmurals-cats-260405-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago native Jennifer Hudson along with Malcolm X, Rosa Parks and the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr., among others appear on a mural on the back of a Humboldt Park building.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Four years later they’ve lost half of their original crew, the duo says.</p><p>“The original concept of Mural Mafia is, we wanted a band of traveling artists who went around and painted cool stuff. But not many people could commit to something like that,” Menace says.</p><p>For now it’s just the two of them.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Menace and Resa painted a series of murals at Touch Gift Foundation in Chatham.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The pair’s latest Chicago project happened around Thanksgiving 2025 and was commissioned by Keona Owens for the walls of Touch Gift Foundation in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chatham" >Chatham</a>, where she is founder and executive director.</p><p>The foundation is a food pantry, digital media lab, coworking space, a place to borrow clothes for job interviews and much more to help neighbors break the cycle of poverty.</p><p>“We want to be a safe haven for the community,” Owens says.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-de0000" name="image-de0000"></a> <picture entries.author: Genevieve Bookwalter

entries.title: Teen boy charged in crash that killed girl, 15, and injured 2 other teens on Southwest Side
entries.summary: <p>A 16-year-old motorist is facing reckless homicide charges after he plowed into a tree, killing his 15-year-old passenger and injuring two other teen girls last month on the Southwest Side. </p><p>The boy, who is not being identified because of his age, was arrested Thursday and charged with reckless homicide with a motor vehicle in the death of Dreanna Jackson, Chicago police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Officers saw the boy driving a stolen Toyota Corolla at a high rate of speed without headlights on March 9. The boy then lost control of the car and hit a tree in the 6800 block of South California Avenue, police said. </p><p>Jackson, who was a passenger in the vehicle was taken to Holy Cross Hospital, where she was pronounced dead at 4:32 a.m., the medical examiner's office said. An autopsy the next day determined she died of multiple injuries from the crash. </p><p>Another 15-year-old girl who was also inside the vehicle suffered a broken arm and a 16-year-old girl suffered an injury to her head. Both were taken to Comer Children’s Hospital, police said. The older girl was in serious condition.</p><p>Police said the Toyota was reported stolen.<br></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Spend in the Black Marketplace seeks to ‘Resurrect the Black Dollar’ this Easter weekend
entries.summary: <p>Kharyn Beard prioritizes the Black dollar. It’s why she’s kept her Auburn Gresham store open for 21 years this summer.</p><p>Beard will be participating as a vendor for a second year at the Spend in the Black Marketplace. It takes place at 75th Street & King Drive from 10 a.m. to 6 p.m. Saturday. Spend in the Black is organized by Charlie E. Dates, senior pastor of Progressive Baptist Church and Salem Baptist Church, in partnership with Ald. William Hall (6th). The event brings together residents and Black entrepreneurs to advance health and financial wellness. It is free and open to the public.</p><p>For Beard, an event like this sends a signal within and beyond the Black community.</p><p>“We spend. Our dollars are important,” Beard said. “I don't even think we realize how important our dollars are. Where we spend our money directly impacts the strength and stability of our communities.”</p><p>Last year’s event produced an economic impact of $1 million in spending and attracted more than 5,000 visitors, organizers said. This year’s event will feature a fresh market experience and health and wellness activations, which include on-site screenings and resources to expand access to care. There will also be live entertainment, music, giveaways, and family friendly programming throughout the day.</p><p>Beard is the owner of KhamrynB, a boutique offering casual to formal wear, both ready-to-wear and custom-made. Her second location is in Roosevelt Collection in the South Loop. She’s participated in a few programs to strengthen her business, like the Mastercard Start Path program that helps small businesses access resources to thrive. Beard also worked with Our United Village, a program exclusively for a group of businesses selected by Beyonce’s foundation BeyGOOD.</p><p>After a profitable day at last year’s Spend in the Black Marketplace, Beard decided to participate again at Saturday’s event.</p><p>“The people came out, they were so excited to have us there,” Beard said. “They were supporting, and they weren't just there looking. People were really spending money.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-820000" name="image-820000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9ab911f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fb5%2F36ac7f2549f7829feab8459ba127%2Fblackspending-260403-017.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e060f1d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fb5%2F36ac7f2549f7829feab8459ba127%2Fblackspending-260403-017.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Owner Kharyn Beard stands in Kham’ryn B Shoes & Accessories in the Roosevelt Collection shopping center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The event’s goal calls for the revival of Black economics, which Dates said can help communities flourish and see less crime. He referenced community divestment as a root cause for teen takeovers like the March 25 downtown incident.</p><p>“One of the ways in which people's lives are made richer and better is through economic empowerment,” Dates said. “If you look at the teen takeover from [March 25], you can see that when a community has been divested or has intentionally been neglected, it produces a sense of despair and hopelessness within the children of that community.”</p><p>This year’s event looks to strengthen both individuals and the businesses that participate. There will be an hourly raffle where the attendees can receive new savings accounts funded with $1,000 to promote long-term financial stability, sponsored by BMO.</p><p>Hall said bringing this event to the once-thriving 75th Street corridor can help the community reimagine that reality once more. “Every ward in every part of this city has its own downtown businesses strip corridor,” Hall said. “This was ours, and the economics are still there. Spending in the Black, I believe, is planting seeds of faith because we're believing for a corridor that will draw people east, and west.”</p><p>Hall said the event puts on display “potential partners in [Chicago’s] $16 billion economy. And so what we want people to walk away with is knowing that there is excellent trade and commerce beyond downtown,”.</p><p>The city’s Department of Business Affairs and Consumer Protection has also made an investment to prepare those businesses to be ready for a profitable partnership. One business will receive $15,000 in seed funding from the event’s business competition. It will also include mentorship to support growth.</p><h3><b>DEI rollbacks and Self-Determination</b></h3><p>In his second term, President Donald Trump has influenced DEI rollbacks across private and public institutions and companies. In response, Black churches have not only vocalized how the rollbacks stunt Black progress, but the churches have also encouraged Black Americans to be self-determined and consciously spend their dollars. Dates said that begins with good money habits.</p><p>“From last year’s Spend in the Black, one of the vendors got connected with resources that enabled her to open a brick-and-mortar flower shop in our city,” Dates said. “So, there are opportunities for the business owners, but there are also opportunities for good spending habits for the people who come.”</p><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Owner Kharyn Beard pins a suit onto a mannequin at Kham’ryn B Shoes & Accessories in the Roosevelt Collection shopping center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While businesses like Beard’s boutique make a profit from pop-ups like Spend in the Black, Black businesses still need more.</p><p>“We don't have money, we don't have access,” Beard said,</p><p>She said that white business owners have easier access to capital, which is hard for Black-owned businesses to leverage. With an infusion of cash, Beard said she could make operational investments in technology, hire and train her employees, and possibly hire support to manage her business’s finances.</p><p>Beard said the lack of support has consequences. “A lot of small businesses are not taught proper banking,” she said. “We're not taught how to make our books look the best way, so that we are financially sound to ask for money from major institutions.”</p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson

entries.title: Desestiman caso contra estudiante de DePaul acusado de escupir a agente de ICE en el aeropuerto
entries.summary: <p>La fiscal superior del Condado de Cook desestimó abruptamente un caso de felonía contra un estudiante de la Universidad DePaul acusado de escupir a un agente del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) durante el fin de semana en el Aeropuerto O’Hare.</p><p>En un informe de arresto, la Policía de Chicago dijo que Brett Heier “intencionalmente y con fuerza” <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/02/acusan-a-estudiante-de-depaul-de-escupir-a-agente-de-ice-en-ohare-pero-abogado-dice-que-el-caso-es-exagerado" target="_blank" >escupió al agente de ICE</a> el domingo, mientras salía de la Terminal 2 de O’Hare. Heier, de 20 años, fue acusado de agresión agravada contra un oficial de la paz y se ordenó su liberación a la espera del juicio durante su primera comparecencia en la corte el miércoles.</p><p>Para el jueves por la tarde, la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, Eileen O’Neill Burke, “había revisado personalmente el caso y ordenado a los fiscales desestimar el cargo”, según un comunicado emitido por su oficina.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a40000" name="module-a40000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Este movimiento inusual ocurre mientras O’Neill Burke enfrenta crecientes llamados para investigar y procesar a los agentes de inmigración que llevaron a cabo una agresiva campaña de deportaciones en el área de Chicago.</p><p>El comunicado no proporcionó una explicación para desestimar el caso, limitándose a afirmar que la oficina de O'Neill Burke "evalúa continuamente la evidencia y revisa los casos presentados por las autoridades para asegurar que las decisiones sobre cargos sean apropiadas".</p><p>El abogado de Heier, Adam Sheppard, insistió en una entrevista con el Sun-Times que el caso estaba siendo exagerado. Sheppard argumentó que Heier solo fue acusado de escupir en el zapato del agente y podría haber sido sin intención.</p><p>“Es reconfortante saber que la Oficina de la Fiscal del Estado del Condado de Cook ejercerá la debida discreción procesal”, dijo Sheppard después de que se desestimó el caso.</p><p>El presunto incidente de escupitajo en O’Hare ocurrió una semana después de que el presidente Donald Trump anunciara que estaba <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/24/despliegan-a-agentes-de-ice-en-ohare-mientras-continua-el-cierre-parcial-del-gobierno-que-afecta-a-viajeros" target="_blank" >enviando agentes de ICE a aeropuertos</a> de todo el país para ayudar con la seguridad en medio de la disputa por el financiamiento del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS).</p><p>El cierre del gobierno resultante de esa pelea por el financiamiento llevaron a largas filas en los aeropuertos y problemas de personal entre los agentes de la Administración de Seguridad en el Transporte (TSA), quienes no recibieron pago desde mediados de febrero hasta que Trump firmó un <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/travel/2026/03/26/tsa-travel-airports-dhs-ice-ohare-federal-budget-congress-impasse-government-shutdown" target="_blank" >decreto ejecutivo que les dio pago retroactivo</a> la semana pasada.</p><p>Los empleados de otras agencias de DHS, incluyendo ICE y Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP), han continuado recibiendo pago porque están siendo financiados por el proyecto de ley de políticas internas de Trump aprobado durante el verano.</p><p>El cierre ocurrió luego de los llamados sin respuesta de los demócratas a reformas después de que dos ciudadanos estadounidenses fueran asesinados durante una operativo de cumplimiento de inmigración en Minnesota a principios de este año.</p><p>O'Neill Burke ha dicho que está legalmente restringida en cuanto a apuntar a los federales que llevaron a cabo la campaña anterior en el área de Chicago, incluso cuando se han lanzado esfuerzos legales para <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/10/abogados-de-chicago-lideran-esfuerzo-para-nombrar-a-fiscal-especial-que-investigue-a-agentes-de-inmigracion" target="_blank" >instalar un fiscal especial</a> en su oficina para hacer precisamente eso.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Cubs right-hander Cade Horton exits Friday's start with right forearm discomfort
entries.summary: <p>CLEVELAND — Cubs right-hander Cade Horton exited Friday's start against the Guardians in the second inning with right forearm discomfort. </p><p>His early exit show the fragility of major-league pitching. Horton walked Kyle Mozardo on five pitches and was in a 1-1 count against Bo Naylor before he called for the dugout. Horton’s final pitch was clocked at 93.8 mph.</p><p>The Cubs have to be hoping for nothing serious to be wrong with Horton, who has immense potential to lead the rotation in the future, but also this season. After a shaky spring training, Horton, the No. 7 pick in the 2022 MLB Draft, looked strong in his 2026 debut against the Nationals. He went 6 1/3 innings, allowing two runs on four hits with one walk and four strikeouts. His performance was a continuation of how Horton pitched last season before a non-displaced rib fracture prematurely ended his season. </p><p>The Cubs have the pitching depth to survive a short absence from Horton, but their ceiling is undoubtedly lowered without him. Horton had a 1.03 ERA over 12 second-half starts, last season. <br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Illinois' Final Four marching orders: Have fun, spill guts, no regrets
entries.summary: <p>INDIANAPOLIS — As anyone who has competed in an Olympics, a Super Bowl, a Game 7 or a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Final Four</a> will tell you, there’s something about the grandest events in sports that’s just different.</p><p>And not “good” different, either.</p><p>The big stage can mess with a player’s head like nothing else.</p><p>It’s the giant fan interest. And the giant media throngs. And the giant pressure. In the case of this men’s Final Four, it’s the giant football stadium, home of the NFL Colts. It’s the puzzling way these winner-take-all situations tend to go by so fast — too fast, a blink — that certain players and teams get swallowed in the moment, left to regret not having done the best they could.</p><p>It’s all those things, and more.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/28/illinois-iowa-final-four-wagler-stirtz-underwood-mccollum-ncaa-tournament-elite-eight-march-madness" >Illinois Final Four-bound for first time since 2005 after beating Iowa in South Region final</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/29/illinois-michigan-big-ten-final-four-championship-drought-michigan-state-spartans-mateen-cleaves-tom-izzo-2000" >Illinois, Michigan give Big Ten two shots at ending quarter-century-long title drought. Is this the year?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/29/illinois-uconn-final-four-illini-huskies-mullins-hurley-underwood-arizona-michigan-march-madness-indianapolis" target="_blank" >Illinois will face UConn in Final Four in rematch of Huskies’ November victory</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Something like this happened to Illinois two years ago in the Elite Eight, when the Illini were blown out by a UConn colossus on its way to a second straight championship. Marcus Domask hit a jumper to tie it 23-23. Then Justin Harmon made a layup, only it came 10 minutes of game time later and made the score 53-25. Incredibly, the Illini had been outscored 30-0 in between baskets. How on Earth does that happen?</p><p>“You definitely could see moments where it was deflating them,” said Alex Karaban, a starter on that Huskies team and now a senior linchpin whose personal NCAA Tournament record is a wildly good 17-1. “It was such a special run. Score, stop, score. And then celebrate after.”</p><p>The Illini who will take the floor Saturday inside cavernous Lucas Oil Stadium simply can’t let anything like that happen again. It’s too miserable a feeling. Win or lose Saturday in a national semifinal against UConn (33-5), the Illini (28-8) have to puff out their chests, bow their necks and then spill their guts. And maybe even have fun doing it.</p><p>The great Illini team that lost the national-title game to North Carolina in 2005 was unable to locate its very best effort when it mattered most.</p><p>“The one that’s going to haunt you forever,” Dee Brown, one of the stars of that team, said last year at a 20th reunion in Champaign.</p><p>Illinois athletic director Josh Whitman was the picture of calm and confidence Friday as he watched the team practice with thousands of fans in the stands and hundreds of media and other workers inhabiting the perimeter of the court. Whitman always projects calm and confidence, which must be one of the secrets to his success since returning to his alma mater in 2016.</p><p>“I’ll be nervous tomorrow,” he said. “Today, we really emphasized trying to be enjoy the moment we’re in. But we’ve got more work to do, got a couple of big games in front of us. I’ve got a lot of confidence in these guys and our coaching staff. They’ll be ready.”</p><p>The sooner they start locking in, the better, and that goes for all the teams here because there are distractions around every corner at the Final Four.</p><p>One would think Arizona coach Tommy Lloyd is laser focused on Michigan, but he did manage to squeeze in a nice contract extension, through 2031, before clocking in for press-conference duty Friday. When there’s a huge game the next night and your coach is announcing, “I’m staying!” and discussing the North Carolina opening he could have filled, you scratch your chin just a little.</p><p>UConn coach Dan Hurley’s top assistant, Luke Murray — actor and Cubs fan Bill’s son — is halfway out the door to Boston College, where he is the new head coach. Murray has been tasked with scouting Illinois and UConn for a potential meeting on Monday. Meanwhile, he’s trying to pull together a roster at BC, which would be hard and chaotic enough under ideal conditions in these wild-west college sports times.</p><p>“He doesn’t sleep anyway,” Hurley cracked.</p><p>Hurley knows the Final Four like the back of his hand. Illinois coach Brad Underwood is coaching in it for the first time. Only a newbie would sit in front of a microphone and share, as Underwood did, how “taken aback” he was by the quality of pillowcases and blankets at the team hotel.</p><p>“I grew up watching this event,” Underwood said. “You’re the kid that’s in your driveway shooting hoops and you’re going to hit the game-winner in the national championship game. … “You watch it and you dream.”</p><p>But it’s no dream now.</p><p>There are guts to spill.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: White Sox surprise Ozzie Guillen by retiring his No. 13
entries.summary: <p>There have been few things in baseball that have reduced Ozzie Guillen to tears. This was one of them.</p><p>The long-time <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> player, World Series-winning manager and beloved broadcaster was caught by surprise Friday during the early innings of the CHSN telecast when one of his former players on the 2005-title winning team, Scott Podsednik, read from a statement announcing that the ballclub was retiring Guillen’s No. 13.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Ozzie Guillen tears up while learning the Chicago White Sox will retire his number later this season <a href="https://t.co/bBU1FP5zkN">pic.twitter.com/bBU1FP5zkN</a></p>— Awful Announcing (@awfulannouncing) <a href="https://twitter.com/awfulannouncing/status/2040147233929113955?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 3, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The number will be retired in pregame ceremonies on Aug. 8, the club announced.</p><p>“That’s one of the biggest surprises I’ve ever had in baseball,’’ said Guillen, of an honor that gained impetus last year during the 20th anniversary celebration of the 2005 team, when numerous former players said he deserved it.</p><p>“I guess [the White Sox] did it the right way, because they made me cry,’’ Guillen said. “I know how important this moment is for my family, myself. That shows you, shows myself that the White Sox cared what I did for them.’’</p><p>Guillen, who began his big-league career with the White Sox in 1985, said he originally asked for the number 9, but was told he could not have it because it had been worn by Minnie Minoso and was retired. Guillen then asked for No. 13, worn by his baseball hero and fellow Venezuelan shortstop, Davey Concepcion of the Reds.</p><p>He said he hopes that Concepcion, who will be 78 this summer, will be able to attend the retirement ceremony. He also is looking forward to the team’s 90-year-old owner, Jerry Reinsdorf, being there as well. “I just talked to Jerry a few minutes ago,’’ Guillen said. “He said, ‘I did want to see you cry once.' ’’</p><p>Guillen has made a lasting impact on the organization in multiple ways.</p><p>“I’ve been lucky in Chicago, man,’’ he said. “A lot of people knew me as a manager, a lot of people knew me as a manager, and a lot of kids know me as that crazy guy on TV.’’</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true"
