entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Will the Blackhawks' penalty kill remain a team strength next season?
entries.summary: <p>SEATTLE — When the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' penalty kill was dominating opposing power plays earlier this season, players were quick to credit coach Jeff Blashill's effective system for its success.</p><p>But Blashill, in turn, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/14/blackhawks-penalty-kill-nhl-system-change-jeff-blashill-connor-murphy" target="_blank" >quick to credit</a> the Hawks' excellent penalty-killers for its success. </p><p>That latter attribution seems accurate in retrospect, considering how substantially the unit has worsened since two of its three most important members — Connor Murphy and Jason Dickinson — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/02/blackhawks-oilers-contrast-connor-murphy-jason-dickinson-winning" target="_blank" >were traded</a>, leaving only Ilya Mikheyev behind.</p><p>The Hawks' penalty kill somehow still ranks No. 1 in the NHL with an 83.6% kill rate, but since the March 6 trade deadline, they rank 27th at 73.8%. They've allowed a goal in nine of 15 games during that span, including the last four straight.</p><p>Some of those goals have been unlucky, such as Matt Savoie's power-play tally <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/02/blackhawks-oilers-playoff-contention-eliminated-landon-slaggert-adam-gajan" target="_blank" >Thursday</a> for the Oilers, owners of the league's best power play. It deflected in off Hawks defenseman Alex Vlasic's skate in the crease.</p><p>But the stats mirror the results. Through March 5, the Hawks' penalty kill had allowed the sixth-fewest shot attempts and ninth-fewest shots on goal per minute. Since then, they've allowed the 12th-most shot attempts and second-most shots on goal per minute.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Matt Savoie - Edmonton Oilers (15)<br>Power Play Goal <a href="https://t.co/zOYJzCtu1q">pic.twitter.com/zOYJzCtu1q</a></p>— NHL Goal Videos (@NHLGoalVideos) <a href="https://twitter.com/NHLGoalVideos/status/2039951540736110733?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 3, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Among the forwards, Dickinson's departure forced Ryan Greene into a bigger role. Analytics show Greene has struggled, allowing the most shots and goals per minute among regular killers.</p><p>Among the defensemen, Murphy's departure transferred more duties onto Louis Crevier, Artyom Levshunov (pre-injury) and now Ethan Del Mastro (post-Levshunov injury).</p><p>On Thursday, the Hawks did generate a breakaway and two-on-one rush shorthanded, but the Oilers' power play nonetheless produced nine shots on goal.</p><p>Del Mastro allowed one seam pass through him that Murphy probably would've blocked. Crevier later got away with aggressively joining a rush, but he looked discombobulated upon returning to the defensive zone and ended up losing his position.</p><p>On Tuesday, the Jets' power-play goal resulted from Landon Slaggert's poor decision to pass backward on a rush, which Blashill dismissed as an individual error. But Blashill did examine more closely what allowed the Jets to generate two consecutive shot-tips.</p><p>Blashill has opted not to tweak the overall penalty-killing system, though, after evaluating what new weaknesses might be created by any adjustments to cover current weaknesses.</p><p>For clarification, the Hawks employ a variation on a "push-down" system with two defensemen stationed near the net while two forwards skate around and pressure the puck.</p><p>"Penalty kill is one of those things where there’s just so many different nuances," Blashill said recently. "We certainly have our structure, but I don’t know any way to learn it without going through it. And so they’re getting those reps."</p><p>Said Slaggert: "It comes down to execution and playing on our toes. When we force them to make plays under pressure, we have a lot of success. If we continue to do that, we'll get right back to where we were."</p><p>All of this raises a far more important question: Will the Hawks' penalty kill remain a team strength next season?</p><p>If it doesn't, the Hawks' 26th-ranked power play will have to improve by an equivalent amount to pick up the slack.</p><p>One X-factor is whether Mikheyev, who has become one of the best killers in the league, re-signs or not. General manager Kyle Davidson has publicly stated his desire to do that, but no contract offer has been accepted yet.</p><p>Aside from Mikheyev, the personnel isn't likely to change much over the summer, so these young killers will have to improve. The Hawks' quest to find a new shutdown defensive center (essentially a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/25/blackhawks-young-forwards-defensive-anchor-jason-dickinson-ryan-donato" target="_blank" >Dickinson replacement</a>) within their young forwards intertwines with this discussion.</p><p>Blashill's system, which previously worked wonders with the Lightning, will be put to the test, too. Exactly how much of the credit does it deserve?</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Illinois remains abortion ‘safe haven’ for out-of-state patients in 2025, report shows
entries.summary: <p>Illinois, by far, remained the most popular destination nationally among patients who had to cross state lines to obtain abortion services in 2025, according to a new report from a prominent abortion-rights organization</p><p>Illinois-based providers performed nearly one out of every four abortions for patients seeking the procedure outside of their home state, often as a result of outright abortion bans or prohibitive restrictions where they live.</p><p>The data from the Guttmacher Institute shows no other state came close to Illinois in terms of volume last year, with providers here performing the procedure at a rate nearly double the state with the next highest level of abortions on out-of-state patients, North Carolina.</p><p>Despite seeing fewer abortions overall, Illinois being the go-to state for out-of-state people seeking abortions has been a relatively <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/abortion/2025/04/17/out-of-state-abortion-patients-illinois-chicago-guttmacher-institute-roe-v-wade" >stable</a> occurrence since 2023, a year after the U.S. Supreme Court overturned its longstanding Roe v. Wade decision that affirmed abortion rights.</p><p>“They come from Indiana, Wisconsin, Tennessee, Kentucky and Iowa,” said <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/07/23/planned-parenthood-of-illinois-announces-new-president-amid-the-nonprofits-fight-to-keep-medicaid-funds" >Adrienne White-Faines</a>, president and CEO of Planned Parenthood of Illinois. “These are neighboring states that also tend to have some limitations and restrictions on abortion care.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6b0000" name="html-embed-module-6b0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28366818"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28366818/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>With regard to abortion access, Illinois stands out from those states.</p><p>Since two-term Democratic <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/10/24/pritzker-illinois-will-never-back-down-on-protecting-reproductive-health-care" >Gov. JB Pritzker</a> took office in 2019, he has moved to preserve and strengthen access to abortion rights in Illinois even as the U.S. Supreme Court embraced tighter restrictions on the procedure and gave states authority to impose total bans.</p><p>The state’s billionaire governor also has helped fund abortion-rights initiatives in Ohio, Florida, Missouri, Virginia and four other states through a tax-exempt organization he created called Think Big America.</p><p>Pritzker's office did not offer an immediate response to the Guttmacher analysis on Friday.</p><p>In 2025, 32,490 abortions were performed on out-of-state patients by Illinois providers, Guttmacher reported.</p><p>That figure is down from 39,890 abortions on out-of-state patients in 2023.</p><p>Last year's total amounted to almost 23% of the 142,310 people who left their home states in order to obtain abortions.</p><p>White-Faines said patient data from 2024 to 2025 from her organization’s clinics across the state bear out that pattern.</p><p>At Planned Parenthood’s Carbondale facility, she said 90% of patients receiving abortions came from Tennessee and Kentucky, states that ban abortions except in instances where a pregnant person’s life is endangered.</p><p>In Waukegan, 46% percent of patients obtaining abortions at the organization’s facility were from Wisconsin. The legality of abortion services in that state was not resolved until a Wisconsin Supreme Court ruling in mid-2025 invalidated an 1800s-era ban on the procedure that kicked in when Roe v. Wade was overturned.</p><p>And at Planned Parenthood’s Champaign offices, 48% of patients were from Indiana, where abortions are banned except in cases of rape, incest and fetal abnormalities or to save the pregnant person’s life.</p><p>“They know that Illinois is a safe-haven state, and they can get access to quality, evidence-based medicine, regardless of the type of care that they desire,” she said. “Whether it's a medical or procedural abortion, they can get easy access to that.”</p><p>Anti-abortion advocates, who have had little success with their agenda at the Democratic-led statehouse, said the new data underscores the need to regulate and license abortion providers, something they say now does not occur under Illinois law.</p><p>“The abortion industry in Illinois is the wild west, which is clear by these numbers,” said Mary Kate Zander, president and CEO of Illinois Right to Life. “When will Pritzker and the state of Illinois start providing real accountability on behalf of vulnerable women? It's disgusting."</p><p>The report also showed an overall decrease in the number of patients traveling for abortion care nationwide, but the number of people seeking telehealth appointments for abortion care has increased in states where restrictions on the procedure are tight.</p>
entries.author: Isabela Nieto | WBEZ

entries.title: Migrante venezolano acusado de matar a la estudiante de Loyola enfrenta cargo federal por armas
entries.summary: <p>El fiscal federal principal de Chicago dijo que no está asumiendo “ningún riesgo” de que el presunto asesino de Sheridan Gorman quede en libertad, y presentó el jueves un cargo por posesión ilegal de armas contra el inmigrante venezolano, ya acusado del asesinato de la estudiante de primer año de la Universidad Loyola de Chicago.</p><p>No se ha programado ninguna fecha de corte federal para José Medina, de 25 años, cuya detención fue ordenada el viernes por un juez de la corte estatal en relación con el <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/23/migrante-venezolano-acusado-de-asesinato-de-la-estudiante-de-la-universidad-loyola" target="_blank" >asesinato de Gorman</a>. El abogado de Medina, en realidad, pidió al juez de la Corte de Circuito del Condado de Cook, D’Anthony Thedford, que mantuviera a Medina en prisión para evitar su deportación.</p><p>Ahora los fiscales federales han acusado a Medina de poseer una pistola semiautomática Smith & Wesson de calibre .40 mientras estaba ilegalmente presente en los Estados Unidos, un cargo que conlleva una pena máxima de 10 años de prisión.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-510000" name="module-510000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Dada la naturaleza insensata y a sangre fría del asesinato de una joven estudiante con un futuro brillante por delante, la oficina del fiscal federal de Chicago no asumirá riesgos de que este perpetrador inmigrante ilegal sea liberado nuevamente en nuestra comunidad”, dijo el fiscal federal Andrew S. Boutros en un comunicado.</p><p>Una queja de ocho páginas alega que la Policía de Chicago encontró la pistola en una residencia sobre Sheridan Road que fue registrada después de que un video captara “a un hombre con ropa negra, usando una máscara negra, cojeando y un caminar distintivo” entrando poco después del tiroteo del 19 de marzo.</p><p>Los testigos describieron al tirador de forma similar.</p><p>Un agente especial de la Agencia de Alcohol, Tabaco, Armas de Fuego y Explosivos (ATF) en el Centro de Inteligencia sobre Armas de Fuego en Chicago escribió que la pistola resultó ser una coincidencia de alta probabilidad con la que fue utilizada en el asesinato de Gorman.</p><p>La queja federal detalla que Medina nació en Venezuela y que no tiene “estatus legal en los Estados Unidos”. Se indica que un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza “encontró” a Medina en el sector fronterizo de El Paso el 9 de mayo de 2023. Medina supuestamente no pudo proporcionar una dirección válida en Estados Unidos pero dijo que planeaba residir en New Rochelle, Nueva York.</p><p>Medina fue liberado bajo palabra de honor el 24 de mayo de 2023 y estaba programado para comparecer ante un juez de inmigración en Nueva York el 13 de octubre de 2023, según la queja.</p><p>Sin embargo, se dice que “no hay indicios de ningún registro de ningún procedimiento relacionado con la inmigración o con ajustes de estatus”.</p><p>El asesinato de Gorman reavivó el debate nacional sobre la inmigración e incluso <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/24/trump-condena-el-asesinato-de-estudiante-de-loyola-mientras-promueve-deportaciones" target="_blank" >atrajo la atención del presidente Donald Trump</a>. Se acusa a Medina de esconderse al final de un muelle cerca de Tobey Prinz Beach y dispararle a Gorman, de 18 años, en la espalda mientras ella y sus amigos corrían a buscar refugio.</p><p>La defensora pública adjunta del Condado de Cook, Julie Koehler, ha dicho que Medina <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/27/sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-jose-medina" target="_blank" >tiene una discapacidad de desarrollo</a> derivada de una herida de bala anterior que sufrió en la cabeza durante un robo en Colombia. Medina llegó a ese país luego de huir de la violencia en Venezuela, dijo Koehler.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-820000" name="image-820000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>José Medina enfrenta cargos locales y federales por la muerte a tiros de Sheridan Gorman, estudiante de primer año de la Universidad Loyola Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Oficina del Defensor Público del Condado de Cook.</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>FALTA PIE DE FOTO</p><p>José Medina enfrenta cargos locales y federales por la muerte a tiros de Sheridan Gorman, estudiante de primer año de la Universidad Loyola Chicago. Oficina del Defensor Público del Condado de Cook.</p><p>Tuvo que volver a aprender a caminar y a hablar, y tiene una hendidura en la parte superior de la cabeza. No puede leer ni escribir, agregó Koehler.</p><p>Medina terminó en Chicago en 2023 después de llegar a la frontera entre Estados Unidos y México en Texas y ser detenido allí, según Koehler. Aunque pidió ser deportado de regreso a Colombia, las autoridades de Texas supuestamente lo enviaron en autobús a Chicago.</p><p>Sin familia aquí, Medina se quedó en un refugio para migrantes y contrajo tuberculosis que no fue diagnosticada, según su abogado. Su madre eventualmente se unió a él aquí, y él vivía con ella en un edificio de apartamentos a una cuadra de donde fue asesinada Gorman.</p><p>La familia de Gorman ha dicho que “entiende” el instinto de compasión.</p><p>“Cada vida tiene una historia”, dijeron en un comunicado la semana pasada. “Pero no podemos perder de vista la simple y devastadora verdad en el centro de todo esto: Sheridan también tenía una vida. Hay una diferencia entre entender una vida y justificar una pérdida”.</p><p><i>Contribuyeron: David Struett, Sophie Sherry </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Top Johnson aide says there will be 'consequences' for CHA power struggle over new CEO
entries.summary: <p>A top mayoral aide said Friday there will be “consequences” for the Chicago Housing Authority’s decision to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/17/chicago-housing-authority-appoints-keith-pettigrew-as-new-ceo" >hire a new CEO</a> whom Mayor Brandon Johnson has never met, after what the administration claims was a secret process that violated the Open Meetings Act.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson's chief of staff, Cristina Pacione-Zayas, refused to say what those consequences would be for a CHA board that bypassed Johnson’s recommendation of former Ald. Walter Burnett (27th) in favor of Keith Pettigrew, who currently serves as executive director of the District of Columbia Housing Authority.</p><p>But she hinted that Johnson was prepared to wage a legal battle with the CHA board that defied him and, as she put it, “knowingly” violated its “oath to uphold the law of the land locally, state as well as federal.”</p><p>“If this board operated in a way that violated one of the key pieces of any public body — and that is the Open Meetings Act to ensure the transparency of all the steps that these officials are required to take when you are fiduciary of public resources — then there will be some consequences around that,” she said.</p><p>“I can’t say what they are, but I can tell you that we don’t have these laws just to say them. They actually do have teeth and they do have expectations… There will be some activity in the coming weeks that you will see and we’ll have to see how this plays out. The mayor stands firm [in his belief that] the process did not follow what is statutorily required and [CHA Board Chair Matthew Brewer] is going to have to answer to that as the chair.”</p><p>Pacione-Zayas compared what she called the CHA’s secret process to the transparent process at the Chicago Public Schools that culminated in the partially elected, partially appointed school board’s decision to give Interim CEO Macquline King the permanent job with a three-year contract at a starting salary of $380,000. </p><p>“It was posted well in advance that a contract would be executed with a named candidate for a special meeting. These are pretty basic guidelines and standards,” Pacione-Zayas said.</p><p>Earlier this week, Johnson took his power struggle for control over the CHA to a new level.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/01/mayor-johnson-oust-cha-board-chair-brewer-agency-vote-new-ceo" >The mayor acted to remove </a> Brewer as board chair and interim operating chairman in apparent retaliation for Brewer’s role in engineering Pettigrew's appointment. </p><p>The CHA board’s 7-2 vote was a political defeat for Johnson, who favored Burnett <sup></sup> and has spent months seeking the federal waivers needed to resolve Burnett’s apparent conflicts of interest.</p><p>Brewer argues that Johnson’s issues with the CHA board’s vote have “nothing to do with violating the law.”</p><p>“They have to do with the mayor not getting the person he wanted as CEO,” he said.</p><p>Brewer said he is “very confident” that the CHA board he chairs “complied with all applicable laws,” including the Open Meetings Act that allows personnel matters to be listed as such and debated behind closed doors. </p><p>“I am a lawyer who hates lawsuits. So I’m never going to say, `Bring it on.’ I’m not picking a fight. I’m not inviting a fight. But if we have to fight, we will fight,” Brewer said.</p><p>The embattled CHA chair maintained that the mayor’s office interviewed Pettigrew about a year ago. It was only then at the end of a search process that dragged on for more than a year that Johnson, as Brewer put it, said, "`I want my guy to come in’ and disregarded this entire process..." </p><p>“It’s ironic that they’re saying that I violated process and I made some unilateral decision," Brewer said.</p><p>Pacione-Zayas questioned how Pettigrew can preside over a $1.4 billion agency that serves 65,000 households and includes some of Chicago’s poorest residents.</p><p>“I’m personally confused. I don’t know how, if you’re a candidate and you have not met the principal that appoints the board — I don’t know you can really move forward. It’s really striking,” Pacione-Zayas said Friday. “If I were the candidate, I would be asking the board, `How come I haven’t met the principal and I’d like to set that up… It’s concerning." </p><p>Burnett’s apparent conflicts of interest, according to the CHA and the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, were with his 30-year record as alderman and longtime ownership of properties rented to housing voucher holders. Since 2007, Burnett and his wife have collected <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/09/12/alderman-walter-burnett-wife-rent-payments-cha-housing-voucher-landlords-hud" target="_blank" >more than $260,000</a> as CHA voucher landlords.</p><p>The board has been unable to consider Burnett, whom many CHA residents opposed, until the agency receives HUD-approved conflict waivers.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Jefe de la Policía de Chicago defiende interacciones de sus oficiales con ICE durante Operativo Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>El superintendente del Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD), Larry Snelling, defiende las acciones de su departamento durante el aumento de la aplicación de la ley de inmigración el año pasado, asegurando a los residentes que sus oficiales estaban “allí trabajando para mantener seguras las comunidades”.</p><p>Snelling se unió a la Comisión Comunitaria de Seguridad Pública y Responsabilidad en una “audiencia especial” el jueves para discutir las interacciones de su departamento con agentes federales de inmigración.</p><p>Docenas de personas asistieron, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/09/residentes-expresan-frustracion-con-policia-de-chicago-durante-redadas-de-inmigracion" target="_blank" >algunos acusando a la Policía de Chicago de coordinar ilegalmente con agentes de inmigración</a> durante la agresiva campaña de deportaciones del presidente Donald Trump.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-750000" name="module-750000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Una reunión similar en enero también estuvo llena de gente, pero en esta ocasión los asistentes escucharon las respuestas de Snelling directamente, lo que generó momentos tensos entre la multitud.</p><div class="Enhancement" Policía de Chicago, Larry Snelling, en la secundaria Thomas Kelly el jueves por la noche.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Nuestros oficiales se presentaron para disminuir la violencia”, dijo Snelling ante un clamor de abucheos del público.</p><p>“Está bien”, respondió Snelling. “Esta es la respuesta que esperaba... Es fácil culpar a CPD cuando buscas a alguien a quien culpar”.</p><p>La reunión terminó con gritos de “CPD, KKK, I-C-E, son todos lo mismo” y varias personas fueron escoltadas fuera del edificio.</p><p>Aunque Snelling defendió firmemente a su departamento, indicó a los comisionados que si se encontrara que los oficiales estaban violando la Ordenanza de Ciudad Acogedora de Chicago, serían responsabilizados.</p><p>La comisión votó el jueves para recomendar a la Oficina del Inspector General de la ciudad realizar una auditoría de posibles violaciones. Es común que la Policía de Chicago trabaje en estrecha colaboración con agencias federales en investigaciones criminales.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" la Comisión Comunitaria de Seguridad Pública y Responsabilidad el jueves.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Utilizaron la tecnología de reconocimiento facial de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) para identificar al hombre acusado de matar a la estudiante de la Universidad Loyola, Sheridan Gorman. Esta semana, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/02/acusan-a-estudiante-de-depaul-de-escupir-a-agente-de-ice-en-ohare-pero-abogado-dice-que-el-caso-es-exagerado" target="_blank" >arrestaron a un estudiante de la Universidad DePaul acusado de escupir</a> a un agente del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en el Aeropuerto O'Hare, aunque los cargos han sido desestimados.</p><p>Sin embargo, las leyes estatal y municipal prohíben a la Policía de Chicago ayudar en la aplicación de la ley de inmigración federal. Aun así, hubo varios incidentes el año pasado que plantearon dudas sobre si se había cruzado esa línea.</p><p>Snelling ha argumentado que se necesita más educación sobre lo que estas leyes significan en la práctica.</p><p>“Nadie escribió la ordenanza ni la ley con este tipo de aplicación de la ley de inmigración en mente”, dijo Snelling el jueves.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> California Ave., el jueves.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>En junio, la Policía de Chicago respondió a una llamada al 911 de un trabajador de ICE solicitando ayuda para el “control de multitudes” frente a la oficina de la agencia en el South Loop.</p><p>Defensores y algunos concejales se enfrentaron a agentes federales después de que al menos 10 personas fueron arrestadas durante revisiones rutinarias de inmigración. Testigos informaron que la Policía de Chicago parecía “hacer guardia” para los vehículos de ICE y acompañar a los agentes dentro y fuera del edificio.</p><p>Los miembros del Concejo Municipal dijeron que el metraje de las cámaras corporales indicaba algún nivel de coordinación con los federales, pero sin una investigación adicional, no podían determinar si se violaron las políticas de santuario.</p><p>Hasta marzo, la Oficina Civil de Responsabilidad Policial (COPA) ha sido encargada de investigar a los oficiales acusados de violar las leyes de santuario de Chicago.</p><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El departamento de Snelling fue criticado nuevamente en octubre pasado cuando la policía de Chicago respondió a las protestas que siguieron a un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza que disparó a Marimar Martínez en el vecindario de Brighton Park. Un comandante inicialmente dijo a los oficiales que se contuvieran, aunque en una conferencia de prensa días después, Snelling argumentó que la Policía de Chicago estuvo presente “durante todo el evento”.</p><p>“A la señorita Martínez le dispararon agentes federales... Cuando eso ocurrió, nuestros oficiales estaban allí en la escena”, dijo Snelling el jueves. “Hubo disturbios civiles. Nuestros oficiales estaban allí para mantener a la multitud separada. ¿Dices que protegimos a ICE? ICE no necesitaba protección”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-790516" name="image-790516"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29f0865/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F01%2F08226aec4b86813e934416c5355d%2Fsnellingice-260403-31.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fd4247a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F01%2F08226aec4b86813e934416c5355d%2Fsnellingice-260403-31.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff3e286/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F01%2F08226aec4b86813e934416c5355d%2Fsnellingice-260403-31.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Los oficiales de seguridad de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago desalojaron por la fuerza a activistas que se mostraron disruptivos durante una reunión especial de la Comisión Comunitaria sobre Seguridad Pública y Responsabilidad. La comisión estaba escuchando al superintendente de la Policía de Chicago, Larry Snelling, en la secundaria Kelly, en 4136 S. California Ave. en el vecindario de Brighton Park, el jueves 2 de abril de 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff3e286/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F01%2F08226aec4b86813e934416c5355d%2Fsnellingice-260403-31.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ec14356/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F01%2F08226aec4b86813e934416c5355d%2Fsnellingice-260403-31.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff3e286/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F01%2F08226aec4b86813e934416c5355d%2Fsnellingice-260403-31.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los funcionarios de seguridad de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago expulsaron por la fuerza a activistas que fueron disruptivos durante la reunión especial del jueves de la Comisión Comunitaria de Seguridad Pública y Responsabilidad. La comisión estaba escuchando al superintendente de la policía de Chicago, Larry Snelling, en la secundaria Thomas Kelly, en 4136 S. California Ave. en el vecindario de Brighton Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Block Club Chicago informó que Snelling se había negado a asistir a la reunión del jueves si se permitía a expertos en reforma policial e inmigración hablar junto a él. Snelling ha dicho que la historia de Block Club es “incompleta” y que, cuando aceptó unirse a la comisión, no había “ningún plan para tener un panel”.</p><p>“Como superintendente de la policía, quiero estar allí para asegurarme de responder todas las preguntas que se están planteando a través de CCPSA”, dijo Snelling a WBEZ el jueves. “Si tenemos un panel allí que no hemos discutido y no sabemos cuál es el formato, las aguas pueden volverse turbias”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Fred Hoiberg named Associated Press men's basketball Coach of the Year after Nebraska's breakthrough season
entries.summary: <p>Fred Hoiberg of Nebraska was named The Associated Press men's basketball coach of the year on Friday following a 28-win season that included the Cornhuskers' first NCAA Tournament win and a run to the Sweet 16.</p><p>Hoiberg, a former Bulls head coach, received 17 votes from a 61-person media panel, edging Duke's Jon Scheyer (13) to become the Big Ten's first national coach of the year since Michigan's Juwan Howard in 2021. Arizona's Tommy Lloyd received 11 votes.</p><p>"It took us some time to get here, but it was all about getting the right players in here, especially the ones that the fans could get behind," Hoiberg said.</p><p>Hoiberg did just that, building a roster that played an exciting style of basketball, locked down defensively and ignited the Big Red fanbase's excitement for the basketball program and upended the notion that Nebraska is just a football school.</p><p>Nebraska went to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in 10 years in 2024 and took a bigger step this season, matching the school record of 26 wins before even getting to March Madness. The Cornhuskers also had a school-record 15 wins in the rugged Big Ten.</p><p>Voting for coach of the year was done before the tournament, where Nebraska posted its first March Madness win in nine all-time tries with its 76-47 win over Troy. The run ended with a loss to Iowa in the Sweet 16.</p><p>"When the sting does wear off, which it will at some point — maybe — these guys deserve a lot of credit for what they have done for Nebraska basketball," Hoiberg said.</p><p>So will the coach with deep roots in Lincoln.</p><p>Hoiberg's grandfather, Jerry Bush, was Nebraska's head coach from 1953-63 and his grandfather from his father's side taught at the school for 30 years. Hoiberg was born in Lincoln and both of his parents are Nebraska graduates.</p><p>Hoiberg played at Iowa State and, after a 10-year NBA career, returned to lead his alma mater to four straight NCAA Tournaments. When he took over at Nebraska in 2019, the Cornhuskers had been to the tournament once in 21 seasons.</p><p>The Cornhuskers went a combined 7-45 in Hoiberg's first two seasons, but he laid the foundation for success. Nebraska went 23-11 to reach March Madness in 2024 and, after just missing the bracket last season, went on a run that riveted Husker Nation.</p><p>Led by sharpshooting Iowa transfer Pryce Sandfort, versatile Dutch big man Rienk Mast and senior point guard Sam Hoiberg — Fred's son — the Cornhuskers got off to the best start in school history, winning their first 20 games, leading to the program's highest ranking in the AP Top 25 at No. 5 with the hometown coach calling the shots.</p><p>"This place means a lot to me," he said.</p><p>Scheyer was runner-up after guiding the Blue Devils to a No. 1 ranking the final four weeks of the season and a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament.</p><p>The Blue Devils' run came as the fourth-year coach adjusted his approach following last year's Final Four run with a perimeter-driven lineup, turning inside to pummel opponents in the paint behind star freshman Cameron Boozer, who became Duke's second straight AP men's national player of the year Friday.</p><p>Arizona spent nine weeks at No. 1 with Lloyd pulling the strings and earned its first Final Four berth since 2001. He was AP coach of the year in 2022, his first season at Arizona.</p><p></p><h3>Voting for AP coach of the year:</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-62a0ac62-2f8c-11f1-89a9-f937192ce997"><li>Fred Hoiberg, Nebraska, 17</li><li>Jon Scheyer, Duke, 13</li><li>Tommy Lloyd, Arizona, 11</li><li>Dusty May, Michigan, 9</li><li>Travis Steele, Miami (Ohio), 9</li><li>Grant McCaslin, Texas Tech 1</li><li>Shaheen Holloway, Seton Hall, 1</li></ul>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Illinoisans push state leaders for more affordable housing options
entries.summary: <p>A <a class="Link" href="https://abundanthousingillinois.org/op-eds/abundant-housing-illinois-backed-housing-affordability-policies-are-hugely-popular/" target="_blank" >YouGov poll </a>conducted last month shows registered voters in Illinois overwhelmingly believe that the cost of renting and buying a home is a problem, think that there aren’t enough affordable homes for average folks and want the state Legislature to take action.</p><p>The poll of 806 Illinois registered voters was taken March 1-9 and has a margin of error of plus or minus 3.8 percentage points. The survey was conducted on behalf of YIMBY Illinois, a nonprofit which “advocates for abundant housing and sustainable, inclusive cities throughout Illinois.” </p><p>But the questions look legit, and the poll even tests some opposing arguments, although no questions about our high property taxes were asked.</p><p>The poll was released as Gov. JB Pritzker and others are pushing a plan to build “missing middle” residences, including multifamily developments and “granny flats” throughout the state. But proponents must overcome strong opposition from many municipal governments, which do not want to give up their control of local zoning.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ba0000" name="module-ba0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>Eighty-four percent of those polled said the cost of renting or buying a home was a “major problem” (47%) or “somewhat a problem” (37%). That majority held up in every state region and demographic. Even 76% of those who approve of President Donald Trump’s job performance said it’s a problem (39% major, 37% somewhat, with 17% saying it’s a minor or not really a problem). Just a reminder that subsets have a higher margin of error than the overall survey.</p><p>Another 67% of respondents said Illinois does not have “enough homes that are affordable for average people to buy or rent,” while 18% said there were enough, and 16% weren’t sure or didn’t know. Again, every region and demographic agreed with the position, including 54% of Trump approvers.</p><p>A sizable 82% said state legislators should “take action” to address the housing shortage and costs of housing in the state (49% strongly agreed and 33% somewhat agreed). Even 68% of Trump supporters either strongly (35%) or somewhat (33%) agreed, while just 20% disagreed (8% strongly).</p><p>Voters were somewhat split when asked to rate their city or town’s efforts to keep housing affordable, with 47% rating it positively, including 3% “very good,” 11% “good” and 33% “acceptable.” Another 40% rated it negatively, with 10% saying it’s “very poor” and 30% calling it “poor.” Those results were roughly similar throughout regions and all other demographics.</p><p>Perhaps the most surprising result was when voters were asked, “even if it isn’t exactly right,” which of the following came closer to their opinions: 61% said it was “more important to build more homes in my neighborhood so that people who work in my community can afford to live here, than it is to protect the neighborhood from change,” while 27% said it’s “more important to protect my neighborhood from change than it is to build more homes that working people can afford.” Even a plurality of 47% of Trump supporters agreed with building more homes in their communities so people could afford to live where they work, while 40% said protecting their neighborhood from change was more important.</p><p>Sixty-five percent said they agreed that “Building more affordable housing is more important for Illinois,” and 27% said “Protecting the character of neighborhoods from change is more important for Illinois” was closer to their opinion.</p><p>And despite a plurality narrowly approving of their local government's ability to keep housing affordable, 65% (including 57% of Trump supporters) said “Illinois state legislators should prioritize building more homes and bringing down housing costs,” while just 18% said “Illinois state legislators should prioritize preserving the power city governments have over what types of homes can be built and where.” Another 17% were unsure.</p><p>The poll also tested various ways of building more affordable housing, and all had majority support, although all had more “somewhat” support than “strong” support.</p><p>Sixty-three percent supported allowing construction of “granny flats” or other accessory dwelling units. Sixty-nine percent supported building townhouses and small apartment buildings “on all-residential lots.” Sixty-six percent said they supported allowing homes to be built on lots that are just 1,500 square feet. Seventy-four percent said faith groups should be allowed to build low-income housing.</p><p>While the numbers don’t show voters want to give the state carte blanche, these results are much more positive than many statehouse types probably expected.</p><p><i>Rich Miller also publishes Capitol Fax, a daily political newsletter, and CapitolFax.com.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-fa0000" name="module-fa0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Rich Miller

entries.title: Portland, Toronto lay foundations in the WNBA expansion draft
entries.summary: <p>Portland, which is welcoming a WNBA team back to the city after 24 years, selected veteran forward Bridget Carleton with the first pick in Friday's expansion draft.</p><p>Guard Julie Allemand, who played last season for the Los Angeles Sparks, was selected by the Toronto Tempo with its first pick.</p><p>The Tempo won a coin flip and opted to take the sixth pick in the college draft on April 13 over the top pick in the expansion draft. So the Portland Fire went first on Friday and will have the seventh pick in the college draft.</p><p>Carleton, who played last season for the Minnesota Lynx and averaged 6.5 points a game, was an unrestricted free agent.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/01/sky-trade-second-round-picks-with-portland-fire-and-toronto-tempo" >Sky trade second-round picks with Portland Fire and Toronto Tempo</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>"Once we finalized our process, and zoomed in on Bridget, and knew we had our first expansion pick, it was obvious we did not want to have Toronto hold our destiny in their hands," Portland general manger Vanja Cernivec said.</p><p>Allemand averaged 5.4 points, 3.7 rebounds and 5 assists in 34 games last season.</p><p>The league's teams protected five players apiece ahead of the expansion draft but those lists were not made public, leading to speculation about which players were available.</p><p>On Wednesday, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/01/sky-trade-second-round-picks-with-portland-fire-and-toronto-tempo" >Sky announced trades with the Tempo and the Fire,</a> which prevented the expansion teams from selecting Sky players. In exchange, the Fire got the No. 17 pick in the college draft and the No. 26 pick went to the Tempo.</p><p>The expansion draft had two rounds, with up to six picks for each team in each round. The teams alternated picks, with the Tempo picking first in the second round after the Fire got the first overall selection.</p><p>Teams could only lose two players to the expansion draft. If a player was taken in the first round, a second player from that same franchise couldn't be taken until the second round.</p><p>Following Allemand, the Tempo selected center Nyara Sabally from the Liberty, guard Marina Mabrey from the Sun, forward Aaliya Nye from the Aces, guard Lexi Held from the Mercury, and forward Maria Conde from the Valkyries.</p><p>In the second round the Tempo selected forward Maria Kliundikova from the Lynx, center Adja Kane from the Liberty, center Nikolina Milic from the Sun, guard Kitija Laksa from the Mercury, and guard Kristy Wallace from the Fever.</p><p>After Carleton, the Portland Fire selected guard Carla Leite from the Valkyries, center Luisa Geiselsoder from the Stars, forward Emily Engstler from the Mystics, guard Maya Caldwell from the Dream and forward Chloe Bibby from the Fever.</p><p>In the second round Portland took guard Haley Jones from the Wings, forward Nyadiew Puoch from the Dream, guard Sara Ashlee Barker from the Sparks, guard Sug Sutton from the Mystics and guard Nika Muhl from the Storm.</p><p>Mabry was also an unrestricted free agent. Each team was allowed to pick only one unrestricted free agent.</p><p>Portland previously had a WNBA team, also called the Fire, that played from 2000 to 2002.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Illinois Accountability Commission requests testimony from Trump officials responsible for Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>The Illinois Accountability Commission wants current and former officials responsible for Operation Midway Blitz to testify at two hearings as part of an ongoing probe of tactics used by federal agents.</p><p>The letters, sent Friday by the Commission’s Chair Judge Rubén Castillo, were to: Stephen Miller, White House deputy chief of staff for policy; Tom Homan, White House “Border Czar;" Kristi Noem, former Secretary of Homeland Security; Gregory Bovino, former “Commander at Large” of Customs and Border Patrol and others, according to Gov. JB Pritzker's office. </p><p>“I established the Illinois Accountability Commission to preserve the truth and document how (President) Donald Trump and his accomplices violated the rights of Illinoisans and terrorized our communities during Operation Midway Blitz. These officials should answer directly to the people of Illinois for the chaos and violence they unleashed,” Pritzker said. “Regardless of whether these officials are still in their roles or not, the people of Illinois demand accountability from them all.”</p><p>Last October, Pritzker signed an executive order that established the commission to hold federal immigration agents accountable for their actions being in the Chicago area during Trump's administration's ongoing deportation campaign.</p><p>"The people of Illinois deserve to know how this operation was planned, authorized, and carried out — and to identify who was responsible for the decisions that led to its implementation in our state,” Castillo said. “The Commission’s work seeks to determine who authorized these actions, what safeguards were in place to protect Illinois residents, and what accountability mechanisms exist when federal operations harm communities.”<br></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: White Sox fans optimistic at home opener despite season's rocky start: 'Hope never dies'
entries.summary: <p>Despite years of ineptitude and a rough start this year, many White Sox fans who showed up for Friday's home opener arrived with a mix of nostalgia for the past and hope for the future.</p><p>Brothers Michael and Steve Malczewski began attending Opening Day as young boys, when their father would pull them out of school — a tradition they later passed down to their own children. </p><p>On Friday, the brothers carried on that legacy, standing outside Rate Field grilling with family and friends, sharing a sense of optimism about the season ahead. </p><p>“We’ve got to get it right this year,” said Michael Malczewski, 53, of Homer Glen. </p><p>The season's first home game against the Toronto Blue Jays, originally scheduled Thursday, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/white-sox-home-opener-postponed-rain" target="_blank" >pushed back</a> a day due to heavy rain. The Sox were already off to a rocky start to the season with a 1-5 losing record, but Michael Malczewski, like many other fans, were excited to see what the team’s new slugger, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/12/22/we-found-our-guy-white-sox-rebuild-taking-shape-after-signing-munetaka-murakami" >Munetaka Murakami</a>, could bring to the team. </p><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“He was a steal for the [franchise],” Michael Malczewski said of the 26-year-old first baseman from Japan. “There will be some adjustments for the team but he’s our bright spot.”</p><p>Earlier in the week, White Sox TV announcer John Schriffen <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports-media/2026/04/02/white-sox-john-schriffen-nickname-munetaka-murakami-gordon-beckham-chicago-sports-network" >ruffled feathers within the organization</a> after bestowing Murakami with the nickname 'South Side Samurai.' When asked about the controversy on Friday, most fans weren't aware of it.</p><p>Dan Kozlowski, of Crown Point, Indiana, said he was just excited to see Murakami in person, calling this season his first “true test” in Major League Baseball. He said he has been impressed with what he’s seen so far, but added that only time will tell.</p><p>“Hopefully Sox fans are welcoming to him,” said Kozlowski, who was attending his 20th home opener. “He’s already given us three home runs which is what we want to see.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3a0000" name="image-3a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7c13de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5276x2961+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd8%2F547aadb94d92bb1382396207d50b%2Fsox.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/50a0011/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5276x2961+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd8%2F547aadb94d92bb1382396207d50b%2Fsox.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fa8e75/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5276x2961+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd8%2F547aadb94d92bb1382396207d50b%2Fsox.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The White Sox' Munetaka Murakami bats against the Milwaukee Brewers in the seventh inning during his MLB debut on Opening Day at American Family Field on March 26, 2026 in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fa8e75/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5276x2961+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd8%2F547aadb94d92bb1382396207d50b%2Fsox.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/98c52a3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5276x2961+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd8%2F547aadb94d92bb1382396207d50b%2Fsox.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fa8e75/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5276x2961+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F76%2Fd8%2F547aadb94d92bb1382396207d50b%2Fsox.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The White Sox’ Munetaka Murakami bats against the Milwaukee Brewers in the seventh inning during his MLB debut on Opening Day at American Family Field on March 26, 2026 in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Patrick McDermott/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Otherwise, the team's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/munetaka-murakami-chicago-white-sox-debut" >brutal road trip to start the season</a> has looked a lot like the past few years. In their first six games, the Sox have been outscored 52-21, with a team batting average of .192 sitting fifth-worst in baseball and an MLB-high .319 batting average against their pitchers. They’ve also been charged with six fielding errors.</p><p>But on Friday, the home team gave the 33,171 in attendance something to cheer about by rallying in the tenth inning to defeat the Blue Jays.</p><p>Win or lose, Kozlowski said Sox fans would continue to show up for their team.</p><p>“Hope never dies,” he said while wearing a white Papal hat and a jersey with Pope Leo on the back. “We’re still here. We’re going to be here every year.”</p><p>For Christina Ashley of Oak Park, being a Sox fan means sticking together through the good times and the bad times.</p><p>“I’m hoping for a better season but we’re still the best team,” said Ashley, 46, a nurse who called out of work to attend the opener. “This is home.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" April 3, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>However, many fans agreed it was time for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/06/05/jerry-reinsdorf-justin-ishbia-reach-long-term-agreement-that-establishes-a-framework-for-transition" target="_blank" >owner Jerry Reinsdorf</a> to sell the team.</p><p>Reinsdorf, 89, reached an agreement with minority owner Justin Ishbia last year that would establish a framework for Ishbia to obtain a future controlling interest in the franchise. Reinsdorf will have the option to sell that interest to Ishbia starting in 2029 until 2033.</p><p>Kozlowski said the team may be blessed by Pope Leo XIV, a South Sider and Sox fan, but it was time for a new owner to take over.</p><p>“Pope Leo says sell the team, sooner rather than later,” Kozlowski jokingly added.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" Gutierrez said it was a late celebration for his son’s birthday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We just want to see progress, but until we change ownership, that's when we really are going to start seeing change,” said David Gutierrez of Naperville.</p><p>“We need an owner who cares about winning,” he said.</p><p>Gutierrez, 39, has attended every home opener since 2010, a tradition he’s maintained since his sons, Cameron and Jacob, were born. Friday’s game was made a little more special for the family who were celebrating Cameron’s 6th birthday.</p><p>Cameron and Jacob, 4, are learning to be White Sox fans, and Gutierrez said he wants his two boys to grow up watching a winning team.</p><p>“I’m hoping when they're a little older this team will finally be doing better,” he said. “I know there’s better days ahead.”</p><div entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Bears’ Caleb Williams: ‘Iceman’ trademark application is to protect my business interests
entries.summary: <p>Bears quarterback Caleb Williams admitted to not knowing that his “Iceman” nickname was shared by former basketball star George Gervin, but said he will continue trying to trademark his name on apparel to protect his own business interests.</p><p>Williams addressed the matter in a video interview posted Friday by Front Office Sports. Williams said he wasn’t crazy at first about the nickname given to him by his teammates after his cold-as-ice comebacks during the Bears' 11-6 season. He figured the nickname made sense, given Chicago’s frigid temperatures.</p><p>Last month, Williams applied for a trademark on the nickname for the purposes of apparel. Gervin, a four-time professional scoring champion with the San Antonio Spurs, applied for a similar trademark days later. He has been called “Iceman” since before Williams was born.</p><p>In an exclusive interview with the Sun-Times, Gervin said he meant no disrespect to the Bears quarterback, but that he was the “Iceman” when it came to sports.</p><p>Williams didn’t rule out speaking with Gervin in the future.</p><p>“It’s all respect for George,” he said.</p><p>Williams told Front Office Sports that it was important for him, and also for other athletes such as Gervin, to be in charge their own business interests, rather than allowing others to produce apparel with their name on it with no financial consequences. That's why, he said, Williams was applying for the trademark.</p><p>“It’s just to control that aspect of it,” he said.</p><p>The trademark processing is expected to take months before Williams finds out whether it will be approved.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.title: Illinois and other Democrat-led states sue against Trump executive order that tightens mail-in voting
entries.summary: <p>Illinois has joined 23 other states in filing a lawsuit against President Donald Trump's executive order that would restrict mail-in voting to a federal list of eligible voters.</p><p>The executive order, signed Tuesday, directs the Department of Homeland Security to work with states to compile a list of eligible voters, using citizenship records and identification data. The order mandates that the United States Postal Service only send mail-in ballots to registered voters on the DHS list, and threatens criminal prosecution and loss of federal funding to states and individuals who do not comply.</p><p>The lawsuit was filed in Massachusetts in partnership with 23 other Democrat-led states, who argue the constitution leaves the right to administer and oversee elections to states, not the federal government. Illinois Attorney General Kwame Raoul contended the executive order will “disenfranchise voters” and cause “chaos” in elections.</p><p>“President Trump’s executive order is a blatantly unconstitutional attempt to undermine our elections and disenfranchise voters all across the country.” Raoul and Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said in a joint statement earlier this week. “Mail-in voting has always been a secure way to vote, and for many, the only way they can vote. Make no mistake, his attempts to attack free and fair elections will fail because Democratic AGs will use every tool we have to continue to hold President Trump accountable when he violates the rule of law and attacks states’ rights.”</p><p>Attorneys general involved in the lawsuit said the order would drastically alter their states' current election practices a few months ahead of the 2026 midterms in November, leaving states without enough time to properly adapt to the new system.</p><p>Trump has long criticized mail-in voting and pushed for stricter voting regulations over unfounded concerns of widespread voter fraud. At a signing event for the executive order, Trump said the move aims to cut back on “cheating” in mail-in voting, despite there being no evidence of signficant voter fraud through mail ballots.</p><p>“It's horrible what's going on,” Trump said. “I think this will help a lot with elections.”</p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: Illinois-Michigan final would be first NCAA title game between conference foes since 'Miracle' finish in 1988
entries.summary: <p>INDIANAPOLIS — It was 50-50 at halftime, but Oklahoma’s Stacey King wasn’t worried. The game was being played at his high-scoring team’s preferred pace. The Sooners were stacked. They were a No. 1 seed for a reason. Plus, they’d already beaten Kansas twice during the season. No problem, right?</p><p>Big problem. Jayhawks star Danny Manning dominated the second half. Their coach, Larry Brown, did some of his finest work, too. The game slowed to a crawl. The Sooners couldn’t handle it and started chucking up bad shots. The No. 6-seeded Jayhawks pulled off an 83-79 major upset for the national championship in 1988.</p><p>Not that King is still sore about it or anything.</p><p>“Oh, man, that’s one of the regrets of my life,” the longtime <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" target="_blank" >Bulls</a> TV analyst recalled. “I wish I had a do-over. …</p><p>“We got out of character. We got frustrated. But when you get to the last game of the year and everybody is watching, sometimes it’s overwhelming to guys. We forgot what got us there.”</p><p>Basketball fans of a certain age remember that as the “Danny and the Miracles” game. It also was a showdown between conference rivals, then from the Big Eight. Believe it or not, there hasn’t been a national championship game between teams from the same conference since.</p><p>“That really surprises me,” King said.</p><p>But there will be another one if <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/04/03/illinois-uconn-final-four-illini-huskies-underwood-hurley-have-fun-spill-guts-no-regrets" target="_blank" >Illinois beats UConn</a> and Michigan beats Arizona in Saturday’s national semifinals. How big that would be for the Big Ten, which hasn’t had a team win it all since 2000.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/04/03/illinois-uconn-final-four-illini-huskies-underwood-hurley-have-fun-spill-guts-no-regrets" target="_blank" >Illinois’ Final Four marching orders: Have fun, spill guts, no regrets</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>King, a serious <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >college hoops</a> fan, is picking both the Illini — “if they shoot it like they can and don’t go through one of those lull periods” — and the Wolverines.</p><p>And his champ?</p><p>“Michigan’s got to be the favorite, if you ask me,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/QmRNBzgplpk?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Danny Manning's dominant 1988 NCAA title game"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg
